body_md: [[person:frumentius|Frumentius]] and his companion Aedesius arrive in Aksum via a shipwreck; they are taken into the royal household and begin to influence the court toward Christianity. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9.

[[person:frumentius|Frumentius]] serves at the Aksumite court; the young [[person:king-ezana|king Ezana]] is educated and influenced toward Christianity. Frumentius later travels to Alexandria to request a bishop from Athanasius. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9; Athanasius, Apol. ad Const. 31.

Christianity is gaining favor at the Aksumite court; Ezana's early inscriptions use both traditional pagan and Christian invocations, reflecting a transitional period. Evidence: Aksumite inscriptions.

Aksumite coinage shows a shift to overtly Christian symbols after Ezana's conversion (often dated c.340-356), supporting mid-4th-century institutional Christianity. [[person:frumentius|Frumentius]] is consecrated bishop by Athanasius of Alexandria. Evidence: numismatic/curatorial synthesis; Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9.

[[person:frumentius|Frumentius]] (Abba Salama) is the first bishop of Aksum; the Aksumite church adopts a Miaphysite theology aligned with Alexandria; this forms the foundation of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9; Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 2.24.

Tradition (Eusebius) holds [[person:mark-the-evangelist|Mark]] founded the Alexandrian church c.42 AD; the claim is ancient but first attested in 2nd-century sources, not contemporary. Uncertainty: no 1st-century documentation of Alexandrian church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 2.16.

Tradition holds [[person:mark-the-evangelist|Mark]] martyred c.68 and [[person:ananianus|Ananianus]] succeeds; Alexandrian Christianity was initially Jewish. Uncertainty: tradition is post-apostolic and first recorded by Eusebius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 2.24.

Mark traditionally martyred c.68; [[person:ananianus|Ananianus]] leads; Alexandrian Christianity is growing in the large Jewish diaspora context. Uncertainty: earliest documentary evidence for Alexandria is sparse in 1st century. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 2.24; 3.14.
Post-70 Alexandrian Christianity growing; the large Jewish diaspora (post-Revolt) context reshapes the community. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.14.
Alexandrian episcopal succession continues; the city is the intellectual capital of the Roman East. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.21.
[[person:primus|Primus]] bishop; Alexandria is developing as a center of diverse religious thought including Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.21.

[[person:basilides|Basilides]] the Gnostic active in Alexandria c.117-138; Alexandrian Christianity is attested more broadly; gnostic schools compete with proto-orthodox. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.1; 4.7.
[[person:basilides|Basilides]] the Gnostic active in Alexandria c.117-138; the proto-orthodox Alexandrian church contends with major gnostic teachers. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.7.
Multiple Christian groups in Alexandria; [[person:valentinus|Valentinus]] possibly born/educated in Alexandria before going to Rome (~c.136). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.7; 4.11.
Alexandrian episcopal succession continues; gnostic schools remain active; the catechetical school tradition is emerging. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.
Alexandrian church is developing as major intellectual Christian center; gnostic schools (Valentinus, Basilides) challenge proto-orthodox leaders. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.
Alexandria is the most intellectually vibrant Christian center of the 2nd century; the catechetical school develops under [[person:pantaenus|Pantaenus]]. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.10.

[[person:pantaenus|Pantaenus]] heads the catechetical school; [[person:clement-of-alexandria|Clement of Alexandria]] arrives and learns from him; vibrant intellectual Christianity in Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.10-5.11.

[[person:pantaenus|Pantaenus]] leads catechetical school; [[person:clement-of-alexandria|Clement of Alexandria]] (c.150-215) writes Protrepticus and Paedagogus; defining Alexandrian Christian intellectualism. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.11.

[[person:clement-of-alexandria|Clement of Alexandria]] writes his major works; [[person:pantaenus|Pantaenus]] dies; Clement assumes leadership of catechetical school. Evidence: Clement, Stromateis; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.11.

[[person:clement-of-alexandria|Clement]] leads catechetical school; young [[person:origen|Origen]] studies here; Severan persecution (202-203) forces Clement to flee Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.1-6.6.

Severan persecution (202-203) forces [[person:clement-of-alexandria|Clement]] to flee; [[person:origen|Origen]] heads catechetical school at age ~18; his father Leonidas had been martyred. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.1-6.3.

[[person:origen|Origen]] is prolific in Alexandria; [[person:demetrius|Demetrius]] ordains him deacon then disputes his presbyter ordination by Palestinian bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.8-6.19.
[[person:origen|Origen]] writes prolifically with support of patron [[person:ambrose|Ambrose]]; produces the Hexapla and biblical commentaries. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23.

[[person:origen|Origen]] expelled (~232) by Demetrius over ordination dispute; [[person:heraclas|Heraclas]] succeeds Demetrius as bishop; Origen relocates permanently to Caesarea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.26.

[[person:dionysius-of-alexandria|Dionysius of Alexandria]] (bishop c.248-265) called 'Dionysius the Great'; a key theological leader who leads through Decian persecution. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.40.

Decian persecution (249-251) forces [[person:dionysius-of-alexandria|Dionysius]] to flee; Alexandria suffers greatly; he writes extensively on the lapsed and later on the resurrection. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.40-6.42.

[[person:dionysius-of-alexandria|Dionysius]] survives Valerian persecution (257-260); his letters on the aftermath are major sources; his Trinitarian views foreshadow later Arian controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.11-7.26.
Dionysius dies c.265; [[person:maximus-of-alexandria|Maximus]] then [[person:theonas|Theonas]] bishop; the catechetical school continues its influence. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
[[person:theonas|Theonas]] bishop; [[person:peter-of-alexandria|Peter of Alexandria]] will succeed him; the Great Peace allows substantial church development. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.

[[person:theonas|Theonas]] then [[person:peter-of-alexandria|Peter of Alexandria]] bishop; [[person:arius|Arius]] is a young cleric in Alexandria whose views will spark the next century's greatest controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.

[[person:peter-of-alexandria|Peter of Alexandria]] martyred 311 during Diocletian persecution; Meletian schism divides Egyptian church; [[person:arius|Arius]]' views begin to crystallize. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8; De Martyribus Palaestinae.

[[person:alexander-of-alexandria|Alexander of Alexandria]] becomes bishop (~312-328); [[person:arius|Arius]]' teachings cause Alexander to convene a synod (~318) condemning Arius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5; Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.4.

[[person:alexander-of-alexandria|Alexander of Alexandria]] attends Nicaea (325) with deacon [[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius]]; they are leading opponents of Arianism; the Nicene Creed is adopted. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.7; Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.7.

[[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius of Alexandria]] becomes bishop (~328); begins his career of defending Nicene theology against Arian pressure; first exile under Constantine (~335). Evidence: Athanasius, Historia Arianorum.

[[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius]] exiled multiple times by Arian-sympathetic emperors; Julius I of Rome supports him; the 'Athanasius contra mundum' period. Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos.

[[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius]] returns from second exile (346-356); prolific writing period; Alexandria is the stronghold of Nicene orthodoxy. Evidence: Athanasius, De Decretis; De Synodis.
Bishop of Amida attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Paul's Galatian letter (c.48-55 AD) addresses churches in Galatia; Ancyra as the provincial capital is a plausible early contact point. Uncertainty: Galatian letter's recipients debated between N. and S. Galatia. Evidence: Galatians 1:2; Acts 16:6.
Church in Galatia (including Ancyra region) probable following Paul's missionary journey through the region (Acts 16:6; 18:23). Evidence: Acts 16:6; 18:23.
Ancyra church probable; 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Galatia among scattered diaspora Christians. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1; Acts 18:23.
Ancyran church attested; 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Galatia and Pliny's letters (c.111) describe Christians in surrounding Bithynian-Pontic region. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1; Pliny, Ep. 10.96.
Ancyran church suppressed during Diocletian persecution; the Council of Ancyra (314 AD) will directly address apostasy issues in this region. Evidence: Council of Ancyra canons.

The Council of Ancyra (314 AD) addresses apostasy canons; it is a major regional synod that precedes Nicaea. [[person:marcellus-of-ancyra|Marcellus of Ancyra]] becomes an important Nicene theologian. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5; Council of Ancyra canons.

[[person:marcellus-of-ancyra|Marcellus of Ancyra]] attends Nicaea (325) and is a strong supporter of the Nicene formula; his own Christology will later be criticized as verging on Marcellitism. Evidence: Nicaea records; Eusebius.

[[person:marcellus-of-ancyra|Marcellus of Ancyra]] is condemned and exiled (~336) by an Eastern synod for his Christology; he appeals to Julius I of Rome. Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos.

[[person:basil-of-ancyra|Basil of Ancyra]] is bishop; he is a leading Semi-Arian (Homoiousian) who opposes both Arius and the Nicene formula. [[person:marcellus-i|Marcellus]] is briefly restored then re-exiled. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.24.
[[person:basil-of-ancyra|Basil of Ancyra]] leads; Ancyra is a center of Homoiousian theology opposing the strict Arianism of Eudoxius and others. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.13.
Bishop of Antinoopolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Disciples first called 'Christians' at Antioch (Acts 11:26); [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] arrives from Jerusalem, then summons [[person:paul|Paul]] from Tarsus. Uncertainty: tradition of Petrine co-founding is strong but secondary. Evidence: Acts 11:19-26.
Antioch base for [[person:paul|Paul's]] 1st and 2nd missionary journeys; [[person:peter|Peter]] visits causing the Antioch incident (Gal 2:11-14). Evidence: Acts 13-15; Gal 2:11-14.
[[person:paul|Paul]] departs Antioch for 3rd missionary journey (Acts 18:22-23); Antioch is established sending community. Evidence: Acts 18:22-23.
[[person:evodius|Evodius]] serves as first named post-apostolic bishop; [[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius of Antioch]] follows (~c.69-107). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.22.
[[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius of Antioch]] leads; his letters presuppose a monarchical bishop with presbyters and deacons at multiple named cities. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles.

[[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius]] bishops Antioch; his seven letters address Ephesus, Magnesia, Tralles, Rome, Philadelphia, Smyrna, and Polycarp. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.

[[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius]] arrested and transported to Rome for martyrdom (~107-117); his letter corpus is primary evidence for early episcopal order. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.

[[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius of Antioch]] arrested and transported to Rome for martyrdom (~107-117); [[person:heron|Heron]] succeeds as bishop; letters widely circulate. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.22; 3.36.
Post-Ignatian episcopal succession continues; Antiochene church maintains structured life. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.20.
Succession continues toward [[person:theophilus-of-antioch|Theophilus of Antioch]]; the church contends with Marcionite and gnostic influences. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.20; 4.24.
[[person:theophilus-of-antioch|Theophilus of Antioch]] writes apologetic works (Ad Autolycum) defending monotheism and Scripture; combats Marcionite influence. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.24.
[[person:theophilus-of-antioch|Theophilus]] writes Ad Autolycum (c.175-180), the first use of the word 'Trinity' in Christian literature. Evidence: Theophilus, Ad Autolycum 2.15.
Antioch is leading Syrian theological center; [[person:maximin|Maximin]] leads; Montanist controversy debated in Syrian synods c.160s-170s. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.19.

Antioch stable under [[person:maximin|Maximin]]; [[person:serapion-of-antioch|Serapion]] becomes bishop c.190 and writes against Marcionites and rejects Gospel of Peter. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.19; 6.12.
[[person:serapion-of-antioch|Serapion of Antioch]] (c.190-211) bishop; writes on Easter controversy and rejects Gospel of Peter from use at Rhossus. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22; 6.12.
[[person:serapion-of-antioch|Serapion]] continues as bishop; involved in Easter controversy letters; rejects Gospel of Peter circulating at Rhossus. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12.

[[person:serapion-of-antioch|Serapion]] leads until ~211; [[person:asclepiades-of-antioch|Asclepiades]] succeeds; Antioch is a major Syrian see with well-established episcopal structure. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12.
[[person:asclepiades-of-antioch|Asclepiades]] succeeds Serapion (~211); Antioch remains major Eastern see during Severan era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12; 6.21.
[[person:philetus|Philetus]] then [[person:zebinus|Zebinus]] bishop; connections with Origen during his prolific Caesarean period. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.21.

[[person:babylas-of-antioch|Babylas of Antioch]] (bishop c.237-253) reportedly denied Emperor Philip the Arab entrance without penance; martyred under Decius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29; 6.39.
[[person:babylas-of-antioch|Babylas of Antioch]] martyred under Decius (~250); his relics later transferred to Daphne by Gallus Caesar, provoking conflict. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29.

[[person:fabius-of-antioch|Fabius of Antioch]] sympathetic to lapsed Christians in post-Decian controversy; [[person:demetrianus|Demetrianus]] succeeds ~252. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 6.46.
[[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]] becomes bishop (~260); his dynamic monarchianism provokes multiple synods condemning him (268). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-7.30.
[[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]] formally deposed by synod (268); [[person:domnus|Domnus]] succeeds; controversy shaped later Christological language. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30.
[[person:domnus|Domnus]] leads post-Paul of Samosata recovery; Antioch restored to proto-orthodox network; relative peace. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
Antioch under stable succession through late 3rd century; church survived major theological crises. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.

[[person:lucian-of-antioch|Lucian of Antioch]] martyred 312 during Diocletian persecution; his biblical scholarship and theology influenced Arius and the Arian movement. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 9.6.

[[person:philogonius|Philogonius]] bishop at Edict of Milan (313); [[person:eustathius-of-antioch|Eustathius of Antioch]] (c.324-330) emerges as staunch Nicene defender. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5.
[[person:eustathius-of-antioch|Eustathius]] attends Nicaea (325) as leading Nicene voice; deposed by Arian-sympathetic council (~330). Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.7.
[[person:eustathius-of-antioch|Eustathius]] deposed by Arian-sympathetic council (~330); Antioch divided between Nicene and Arian factions. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.21.
Antioch under Arian influence; Nicene minority persists; Meletian schism developing. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.

[[person:leontius-of-antioch|Leontius]] bishop under Arian influence; Nicene Meletian schism developing; Antioch key site of Arian controversy under Constantius II. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.28.
Apamea church attested; Origen is known to have visited Apamea in Syria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Bishop of Apamea attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Tradition holds that Mark the Evangelist sent [[person:hermagoras|Hermagoras]] as first bishop of Aquileia; this is a later hagiographic claim (4th-5th century) not supported by 1st-century sources. Uncertainty: founding tradition is very late and legendary. Evidence: Medieval tradition only.
Aquileian church's apostolic founding tradition is unattested in 1st-century sources; the claim of Markan mission is a later legend. Evidence: No primary source.
Christian presence at Aquileia probable by early 2nd century; the city is the main gateway between Italy and the eastern provinces. Evidence: Plausible inference from trade and military routes.
Aquileian church attested by the mid-2nd century; an episcopal succession exists. Evidence: General records; Ambrose and others reference the Aquileian tradition.
Aquileian bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Aquileia becomes a key western center of Nicene orthodoxy. Evidence: Nicaea records.
[[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius of Alexandria]] takes refuge in the West, including time at Aquileia, during his first exile (340-346). Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos.

[[person:fortunatianus-of-aquileia|Fortunatianus of Aquileia]] (bishop ~343-369) leads; he is notable for later baptizing Julian the Apostate's father and for a newly-rediscovered commentary on the Gospels. Evidence: General records.
[[person:fortunatianus-of-aquileia|Fortunatianus of Aquileia]] continues as bishop; Aquileia is a center of Nicene resistance to Constantius II's Arianism. Evidence: General records.
Arelate church probable; Irenaeus's mission covers Narbonensis region. Evidence: General records.
Arelate church probable; Tertullian references Christian communities in Gaul broadly. Evidence: Tertullian; general records.
Arelate church attested; bishop [[person:marinus|Marinus]] of Arles is martyred under Diocletian (c.304 AD). Evidence: General records.

The Council of Arles (314 AD) is held here — called by Constantine I and attended by bishops from across the Western Empire (Britain, Spain, Gaul, Italy, North Africa); it is the first major Western church council, addressing the Donatist schism. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop of Arelate attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Arelate church attested; the Council of Arles (314 AD) held here is one of the most important early Western church councils, bringing together 16+ bishops from Britain to North Africa to address the Donatist schism. Evidence: Council of Arles records.

Ascalon (Ashkelon) is one of the five Philistine cities of the coastal plain, situated between Gaza and Joppa; Philip the Evangelist evangelizes the coastal plain between Azotus and Caesarea (Acts 8:40), and Ascalon lies along this route; Christian presence probable from the early mission. Evidence: Acts 8:39-40; general inference.

Ascalon church attested; as the major Philistine coastal city between Gaza and Joppa it lies directly on the route Philip the Evangelist travels after baptizing the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:39-40), making it one of the earliest evangelized sites on the Palestinian coast. Evidence: Acts 8:39-40; general records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] preaches at the Areopagus (Acts 17:22-34); limited response but [[person:dionysius-areopagite|Dionysius the Areopagite]] and [[person:damaris|Damaris]] are converts; Paul finds an 'unknown god' altar. Evidence: Acts 17:16-34.

Athenian church continues; [[person:dionysius-areopagite|Dionysius the Areopagite]] is traditionally the first bishop of Athens. Uncertainty: the tradition is late and the identification is debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.4; 4.23.
Athenian church continues; the community was apparently small after Paul's visit but grew in subsequent decades. Evidence: Dionysius of Corinth, in Eusebius 4.23 (references Athens).
Athenian church continuing; [[person:quadratus|Quadratus]] presented his Apology to Hadrian in Athens (~124 AD), one of the earliest Christian apologists. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3.
[[person:quadratus|Quadratus]] presents his Apology to Hadrian in Athens (~124 AD); the Athenian church is attested with an active apologetic mission. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3.
Athenian church attested; [[person:quadratus|Quadratus]]' Apology shows the community engaging with Greek philosophical culture. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3.

[[person:aristides|Aristides of Athens]] presents his Apology to Hadrian (~c.125 AD); the Athenian church has apologists engaging the Greek philosophical tradition. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3.
Athenian church continues; Aristides' Apology circulates as a key early apologetic text. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3.

[[person:athenagoras|Athenagoras of Athens]] (c.133-190) will write the Legatio (~177 AD) defending Christians to Marcus Aurelius; he represents the sophisticated Athenian apologetic tradition. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26 (via reference).
Athenian church continues; [[person:athenagoras|Athenagoras]] writes Legatio (~177 AD) defending Christianity to Marcus Aurelius and Commodus. Evidence: Athenagoras, Legatio.
[[person:athenagoras|Athenagoras]] writes Legatio (~177 AD) and De Resurrectione; sophisticated Athenian apologetics defending Christianity. Evidence: Athenagoras, Legatio.
Dionysius of Corinth writes to Athens (c.170) after a period of persecution had almost wiped out the community; Publius martyred. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
[[person:athenagoras|Athenagoras]] writes Legatio (~177 AD); Dionysius of Corinth's letter notes persecution nearly destroyed the Athenian church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
Athenian church recovers from near-destruction noted by Dionysius of Corinth; continues as established see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.
Athenian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] preach the gospel at Attalia (Acts 14:25) and then sail from there back to Antioch (Acts 14:26), completing Paul's first missionary journey. Attalia is the main port of Pamphylia. Evidence: Acts 14:25-26.
Attalia church continues. Evidence: Acts 14:25-26.
Bishop of Attalia attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Attalia church attested; Paul and Barnabas departed here at the end of the first missionary journey (Acts 14:25-26), marking the completion of the first deliberate outreach beyond Syria and Cilicia. Evidence: Acts 14:25-26.
Augusta Treverorum church probable; Irenaeus's missionary work in nearby Gaul suggests spread toward the Rhine region. Evidence: General inference from Irenaeus's activity.
Augusta Treverorum church attested by early 3rd century; traditional first bishop [[person:eucharius|Eucharius]] is placed in this period. Evidence: General records.

Augusta Treverorum becomes the western imperial capital under [[person:constantius-chlorus|Constantius I]] and later Constantine I (307-316 AD); the city has an important Christian community protected by imperial favour. Evidence: General records.

[[person:constantine-i|Constantine I]] rules from Augusta Treverorum until ~316 AD; he builds a major basilica (the Aula Palatina, c.310 AD) here — one of the largest surviving Roman buildings in the world; also begins cathedral construction. Evidence: General records.
Bishop of Augusta Treverorum attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Augusta Treverorum church attested; Constantine's building program here (Aula Palatina basilica, cathedral) makes Trier one of the most important early Christian architectural sites in the West. Evidence: Archaeological evidence; general records.

Augusta Vindelicorum church attested; [[person:afra|Afra]] of Augsburg is martyred here c.304 AD during the Diocletian persecution — she becomes the patron saint of Augsburg; her martyrdom is one of the best-attested early martyrdoms in the Alpine region. Evidence: Passio Afrae; general records.
Augusta Vindelicorum church attested post-persecution; veneration of Afra begins. Evidence: Passio Afrae; general records.
Bishop of Augusta Vindelicorum attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Augusta Vindelicorum church attested; the martyrdom of Afra (c.304 AD) here is one of the earliest documented Christian martyrdoms in the Alpine-Danubian region and establishes Augsburg as an important early Christian center in what is today southern Germany. Evidence: Passio Afrae.

The Spirit of the Lord 'snatches [[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip]] away' from the road to Gaza after baptizing the Ethiopian eunuch and 'Philip found himself at Azotus (Ashdod)'; he then evangelizes all the cities from Azotus northward to Caesarea Maritima (Acts 8:39-40). Evidence: Acts 8:39-40.

Azotus church continues; Philip's miraculous appearance here after the Ethiopian eunuch episode (Acts 8:39-40) is one of the NT's most dramatic missionary stories and anchors this Philistine coastal city as a very early Christian site. Evidence: Acts 8:39-40.
Barcinonae church attested; Bishop [[person:probus|Probus]] of Barcelona attends the Council of Arles (314 AD) — a documented historical attestation. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop of Barcinonae attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Barcinonae church attested; Bishop Probus of Barcelona is documented at the Council of Arles (314 AD), making this the most historically secure early attestation for the Barcelona see; the city's proximity to the Via Domitia connecting Gaul and Spain makes it a key early Christian waypoint. Evidence: Council of Arles records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:silas|Silas]] preach in Beroia (Acts 17:10-14); the Bereans are praised for examining the scriptures daily. Paul is driven out by agitators from Thessalonica but Silas and Timothy remain. [[person:sopater-of-beroia|Sopater of Beroia]] (Acts 20:4) is an early named Christian from the city. Evidence: Acts 17:10-14; 20:4.
Beroia has an established church following Paul's preaching; [[person:sopater-of-beroia|Sopater of Beroia]] travels with Paul to Jerusalem (Acts 20:4). Evidence: Acts 20:4.
Beroian church attested; praised in Acts for Berean diligence in searching the scriptures. Evidence: Acts 17:11.
Berytus church attested; Origen's students travel to Caesarea via Berytus for studies with him; the city has law students including Christians. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.30.

Berytus church attested; Gregory Thaumaturgus (before his Neocaesarean episcopate) studies at the Berytus law school before being converted by Origen. Evidence: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Address to Origen.
Bishop of Berytus attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Berytus church attested; Gregory Thaumaturgus studied at Berytus's famous law school before Origen converted him — illustrating how the city's educational prominence intersected with early Christianity. Evidence: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Address to Origen.

Bethany is the home of [[person:mary|Mary]], [[person:martha-of-bethany|Martha]], and [[person:lazarus-of-bethany|Lazarus]] — [[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus's]] closest friends outside the Twelve. Jesus raises Lazarus from the dead here (John 11:1-44), his most dramatic miracle before the crucifixion; he is also anointed here (Matt 26:6-13; John 12:1-8); and the ascension tradition is associated with the road from Bethany to the Mount of Olives (Luke 24:50-51). Evidence: John 11:1-44; Luke 24:50-51.
Bethany community continues; Origen references Bethany as a pilgrimage site. Evidence: General records.

Bethany community attested; the raising of Lazarus here (John 11:1-44) is the culminating miracle of Jesus's ministry and the direct catalyst for the Sanhedrin's decision to have Jesus killed (John 11:47-53). Evidence: John 11:1-44.

Bethlehem is Jesus's birthplace (Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7); wise men visit (Matt 2:1-12); the slaughter of the innocents occurs under Herod. An early Jewish Christian community maintains the memory of these events. Evidence: Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7.
Bethlehem community continues; the cave or grotto of the nativity is remembered by local Christians. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dial. 78.
Bethlehem community continues. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dial. 78 (~155 AD) confirms local memory of the birthplace cave.
Bethlehem community continues; Justin Martyr confirms the nativity cave is still remembered. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dial. 78.
Bethlehem community continues; Origen references Bethlehem in his commentaries. Evidence: General records.

Bethlehem community attested; Constantine I and his mother Helena begin planning the construction of holy site churches; the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem is commissioned. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.41-43.
The original Church of the Nativity (commissioned by Constantine and Helena) is dedicated c.333-339 AD, built over the traditional birth cave. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.41-43.
The Church of the Nativity is fully operational; Bethlehem becomes a major pilgrimage destination. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine; Egeria's later account.

Bethlehem is a thriving pilgrimage center; the Church of the Nativity draws Christians from across the empire; Jerome later comes to Bethlehem to live and translate the Bible (Vulgate). Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine.
Bishop of Bononia attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bostra church attested; Origen references the church in Arabia. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Bostra church attested; [[person:beryllus-of-bostra|Beryllus of Bostra]] is becoming an important Arabian bishop. Evidence: General records.

[[person:beryllus-of-bostra|Beryllus of Bostra]] (bishop c.230s-244) holds a heterodox Christology (denying Christ's pre-existence and deity), prompting Origen to travel to Bostra and debate him (c.244 AD); Beryllus reportedly recants. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.33.

[[person:origen|Origen]] debates [[person:beryllus-of-bostra|Beryllus of Bostra]] c.244 AD on Christology; the synod held at Bostra corrects Beryllus's teaching on the pre-existence of Christ. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.33.
Bishop of Bostra attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bostra church attested; Beryllus of Bostra's debate with Origen (~244 AD) is a landmark example of synodal correction of heterodox Christology. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.33.

Tradition holds that Andrew the Apostle founded the church at Byzantium and appointed [[person:stachys|Stachys]] (Romans 16:9) as first bishop. Uncertainty: tradition first attested in late sources; scholarly consensus places earliest attestation c.100 AD. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.4 (via tradition); Romans 16:9.
Tradition continues through [[person:stachys|Stachys]]; no contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: tradition retrospective. Evidence: General tradition.
Byzantine church attested in the episcopal lists; Byzantium is an important strategic city with a functioning diocese. Evidence: Eusebius (implied via episcopal lists).
Byzantine church continues; [[person:metrophanes-of-byzantium|Metrophanes]] will be the first bishop of Constantinople after the city's refounding. Evidence: General records.
[[person:metrophanes-of-byzantium|Metrophanes]] bishop (~306-314); Diocletian siege of Byzantium (306) followed by Constantine's eventual control. Evidence: General records.
[[person:metrophanes-of-byzantium|Metrophanes]] bishop; Byzantine church is established; the city will soon become Constantine's new capital. Evidence: General records.

[[person:metrophanes-of-byzantium|Metrophanes]] then [[person:alexander-of-byzantium|Alexander of Byzantium]] bishop; Constantine defeats Licinius at Byzantium (324) and begins transforming it. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.3.

[[person:constantine-i|Constantine]] refounds Byzantium as Constantinople (dedicated 330 AD); [[person:alexander-of-byzantium|Alexander of Byzantium]] becomes first bishop of Constantinople; attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.48.

Constantinople dedicated 330 AD; [[person:alexander-of-constantinople|Alexander]] bishop; the city is now the imperial capital and the church gains enormous prestige. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.48.

Constantinople church is deeply contested between Nicene ([[person:paul-of-constantinople|Paul]]) and Arian ([[person:eusebius-of-nicomedia|Eusebius of Nicomedia]], [[person:macedonius|Macedonius]]) factions; Paul expelled multiple times. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.7.

[[person:paul-of-constantinople|Paul of Constantinople]] martyred (~351) under Constantius II; [[person:macedonius|Macedonius]] leads the Arian-leaning party; Constantinople becomes an increasingly powerful see. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.2.
Caesaraugusta church attested by late 2nd century; Tertullian references Iberian churches broadly. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.
Caesaraugusta bishop attends the Council of Elvira (c.306); one of the most important early Spanish sees. Evidence: Council of Elvira records.
Bishop [[person:valerius-of-caesaraugusta|Valerius of Caesaraugusta]] attends the Council of Arles (314 AD) — one of the earliest named bishops of Zaragoza. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop of Caesaraugusta attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Caesaraugusta church attested; Bishop Valerius (d.315 AD) makes Caesaraugusta one of the best-documented early Spanish sees; the city's martyrs Vincentius (d.304) and Encratis became major local saints. Evidence: Council of Arles records.

1 Peter 1:1 addresses Cappadocia among scattered diaspora Christians; the region likely has Christians by mid-2nd century. Uncertainty: no specific primary source for Caesarea Cappadocia in this decade. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1.
Caesarean Cappadocian church attested; [[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian of Caesarea]] (bishop c.230-268) studied under Origen. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.26.
Caesarean Cappadocian church attested; [[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian of Caesarea]] will be bishop ~230-268 and a strong supporter of Origen. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.26.
[[person:alexander|Alexander]] of Cappadocia bishop before moving to Jerusalem; [[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian]] studies under Origen c.230. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.11.
[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian of Caesarea]] bishop; he travels to Caesarea Maritima to learn from Origen. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.26.

[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian of Caesarea]] is bishop; [[person:origen|Origen]] visits Cappadocia and teaches; Firmilian brings Origen to Caesarea Cappadocia repeatedly. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.26; 6.27.
[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian]] leads Cappadocian church; he invites Origen to Caesarea multiple times. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.27.
[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian]] leads; Decian persecution broadly affects the church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.46.

[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian of Caesarea]] leads; he participates in the debate over baptism of heretics against Stephen of Rome and in the Paul of Samosata controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28.
[[person:firmilian-of-caesarea|Firmilian]] dies ~268 on his way to a synod on Paul of Samosata; unnamed successors continue. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30.
Caesarean Cappadocian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:basil-of-caesarea|Basil of Caesarea]] (born ~330) will become the great bishop of Caesarea Cappadocia and Cappadocian Father; the region is developing as a major theological center. Evidence: General records.

Caesarean Cappadocian church attested; the Cappadocian Fathers ([[person:basil-of-caesarea|Basil]], Gregory of Nyssa, Gregory Nazianzus) will emerge from this region in the next generation. Evidence: General records.

[[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip the Evangelist]] settles in Caesarea (Acts 21:8); [[person:peter|Peter's]] baptism of [[person:cornelius|Cornelius]] here (~40 AD) is a pivotal Gentile admission event. Evidence: Acts 10; Acts 21:8.

[[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip]] remains in Caesarea (Acts 21:8 shows daughters prophesying decades later); [[person:paul|Paul]] passes through on journeys (Acts 9:30; 18:22). Evidence: Acts 9:30; 18:22.
[[person:paul|Paul]] imprisoned at Caesarea Maritima for ~2 years (~58-60 AD); [[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip the Evangelist's]] four daughters prophesy here. Evidence: Acts 21-27.

Caesarea Maritima is the Roman provincial capital; Christian community continues; [[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip]] likely resident. Uncertainty: no specific documentation post-Acts for this decade. Evidence: Acts 21:8 (implied continued presence).

Caesarea Maritima is developing episcopal see; the Roman administrative importance of the city makes it a natural center. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Palestinian records.
Caesarea Maritima continues as important see in Roman Palestine. Uncertainty: no specific primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Palestinian records.

Caesarea has an established episcopal see referenced in 2nd-century records; important as Roman administrative capital of Judaea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.5 (references earlier Caesarean bishops).
Caesarean church continues in the Roman administrative capital. Evidence: Eusebius references earlier Caesarean bishops.
Caesarean church continues; approaching Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135) which reshapes Palestinian Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5.
Post-Bar Kokhba (135) Caesarea becomes the provincial capital of Syria Palaestina; the church here grows in significance. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12.
Caesarean church continuing; Justin Martyr's connections to Palestine include Caesarea-area traditions. Evidence: General records.
Caesarean church growing in the provincial capital; the see will become important under Origen and Eusebius. Evidence: General records.
Caesarean church continues as part of Palestinian episcopal network. Evidence: Eusebius references Palestinian bishops of this era generally.
Caesarean church continuing; Irenaeus' contacts in Palestine imply an active episcopal network here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.

[[person:theoctistus-of-caesarea|Theoctistus of Caesarea]] is the known bishop of this era; he will later invite Origen to preach, provoking controversy with Demetrius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.

[[person:theoctistus-of-caesarea|Theoctistus]] and [[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander of Jerusalem]] invite [[person:origen|Origen]] to preach at Caesarea (c.215-216), causing conflict with Demetrius of Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
[[person:theoctistus-of-caesarea|Theoctistus]] leads; [[person:origen|Origen]] invited to preach at Caesarea by local bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
[[person:theoctistus-of-caesarea|Theoctistus]] continues as bishop; the Caesarean church plays a key role in Palestinian synods. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23.

[[person:origen|Origen]] settles permanently in Caesarea (~232) after expulsion from Alexandria; founds Christian School of Caesarea; prolific scholarship here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23-6.27.

[[person:origen|Origen]] produces Hexapla and massive commentary corpus at Caesarea; [[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] later builds library here preserving Origen's works. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.24; 6.32.

[[person:origen|Origen]] tortured under Decian persecution (~250) and dies (~254); Caesarea School continues; [[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] preserves Origen's legacy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 6.43.
[[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus of Caesarea]] builds library and transmits Origen's legacy; he will write an Apology for Origen. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.5.
[[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] active at Caesarea; [[person:theotecnus|Theotecnus]] bishop; Caesarea is a major center of biblical scholarship. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
[[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] oversees Caesarean library; [[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius of Caesarea]] studies under him here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.

[[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] and [[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius]] collaborate at Caesarea; Pamphilus writes Apology for Origen; the school is the greatest Christian library of antiquity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.

[[person:pamphilus-of-caesarea|Pamphilus]] martyred in Diocletianic persecution (309-310); [[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius]] witnesses and records Palestinian martyrdoms firsthand. Evidence: Eusebius, De Martyribus Palaestinae.

[[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius of Caesarea]] becomes bishop (~313) and writes his Ecclesiastical History; Caesarea is the premier center of Christian historiography. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. Pref.

[[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius of Caesarea]] attends Nicaea (325) and signs the Nicene Creed after initial hesitation; remains a moderate Nicene voice. Uncertainty: his exact position at Nicaea is debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Vita Constantini; Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.12.

[[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius of Caesarea]] dies c.339; [[person:acacius-of-caesarea|Acacius of Caesarea]] succeeds him and takes an Arian-leaning position. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
[[person:acacius-of-caesarea|Acacius of Caesarea]] (bishop c.340-366) leads an Arian-leaning faction; important figure in post-Nicene controversies. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.

[[person:acacius-of-caesarea|Acacius of Caesarea]] leads Arian-leaning faction; Caesarea has shifted from the center of scholarship under Origen/Eusebius to a contested Arian see. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.

At Caesarea Philippi, [[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus]] asks 'Who do men say that I am?' and [[person:peter|Peter]] confesses 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God' (Matt 16:13-20) — the pivotal moment of Peter's leadership commission and the promise to build the Church on this rock. Evidence: Matt 16:13-20; Mark 8:27-30; Luke 9:18-20.
Caesarea Philippi church probable; Eusebius references a healing statue at Caesarea Philippi attributed to the woman healed by Jesus (Matt 9:20-22). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.18.
Caesarea Philippi church attested; Eusebius writes of a famous bronze statue there commemorating Jesus's healing. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.18.
Bishop of Caesarea Philippi attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Caesarea Philippi church attested; Eusebius of Caesarea personally examines the famous bronze statue there. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.18.

Caesarea Philippi church attested; Peter's confession 'You are the Christ' here (Matt 16:16-18) is the foundational text for Petrine primacy and the promise to build the Church — one of the most theologically significant NT scenes. Evidence: Matt 16:13-20.

Capernaum is [[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus's]] ministry base ('his own city', Matt 9:1; 4:13); here he heals [[person:peter|Peter's]] mother-in-law (Matt 8:14), the centurion's servant (Matt 8:5-13), the paralytic (Mark 2:1-12), and many others; he teaches in the synagogue ([[person:john-the-apostle|John]] 6:59); he calls Matthew/[[person:levi-matthew|Levi]] (Matt 9:9). Peter and [[person:andrew|Andrew]] live here. Evidence: Matt 4:13; 9:1; Mark 2:1; John 6:59.
Capernaum community continues; Peter's house is already being venerated as a domus-ecclesia. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.
Capernaum community continues; the octagonal veneration structure over Peter's house is beginning to be established. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.

Capernaum community continues; archaeological excavations have confirmed an insula (house church) at Peter's house — one of the earliest identifiable Christian worship sites. Evidence: Archaeological evidence; Matt 4:13; 9:1.

Capua (modern Santa Maria Capua Vetere) is the second-most important city of Roman Italy after Rome, located on the Appian Way; Christian presence probable by mid-1st century given its proximity to Puteoli (where Paul landed, Acts 28:13) and constant traffic to and from Rome. Evidence: General inference.
Capua church probable; Paul passes through this region on the way to Rome (Acts 28:13-15). Evidence: General records.
Bishop of Capua attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bishop of Carnuntum area attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Carrhae (Harran, modern Turkey) is the ancient city where Abraham sojourned (Gen 11:31-12:4) on the way from Ur to Canaan; as part of the Edessa network it likely has early Christian presence, and later becomes an important Syriac Christian city. Evidence: General inference; Gen 11:31.
Bishop of Carrhae attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Carrhae church attested; as Abraham's city of sojourn (Gen 11:31-12:4) Carrhae holds deep symbolic significance for Jewish and Christian theologians; its position between Edessa and Nisibis makes it a key node in the Syriac Christian network connecting the Roman and Sassanid worlds. Evidence: Gen 11:31; Syriac chronicles.

North African Christianity is traditionally dated earlier than the Scillitan Martyrs trial (180 AD); a Christian community at Carthage is probable by the early 2nd century. Uncertainty: earliest specific evidence dates to 180 AD. Evidence: Acts of the Scillitan Martyrs (180).
North African Christian community probable; Tertullian is born in Carthage c.160 AD and knows of long-established Christianity. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology.
[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] (c.160-225) is born in Carthage; his writings assume a large established Christian community with its own traditions. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology.
[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] grows up in Carthage; the North African Christian community is well-established by this point. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology.
[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] is growing as a rhetorician; converts to Christianity ~c.193-197; the Carthaginian church is substantial. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology.

Scillitan Martyrs trial held AT Carthage (180 AD) is the earliest documentary evidence of Christianity in North Africa; [[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] converts ~c.193. Evidence: Acts of Scillitan Martyrs (180).
[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] converts (~c.193) and begins prolific writing; the Carthaginian church is large enough to be known to Rome. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20.
[[person:tertullian|Tertullian's]] Apologeticus (197 AD) is a major defense of Christianity addressed to Roman governors; he writes extensively defending Christianity. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology.

[[person:perpetua|Perpetua]] and [[person:felicitas|Felicitas]] martyred at Carthage (203 AD) under Septimius Severus; The Passion of Perpetua and Felicitas is a vivid primary source; [[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] moving toward Montanism. Evidence: Passion of Perpetua and Felicitas.

[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] fully joins the Montanist movement (~c.207-213); writes prolifically from a rigorist perspective; [[person:agrippinus|Agrippinus]] is the earliest named bishop of Carthage. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20.

[[person:tertullian|Tertullian]] dies c.225; [[person:agrippinus|Agrippinus]] is the earliest attested bishop of Carthage; the North African church is the most vibrant Latin-speaking community. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20.
North African church active; [[person:agrippinus|Agrippinus]] convenes synods on baptism of heretics; growing theological independence of North African church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.3.

[[person:cyprian-of-carthage|Cyprian of Carthage]] becomes bishop (~248); a wealthy Roman citizen converted c.246; he will face the Decian persecution and the question of the lapsed. Evidence: Cyprian, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.43.

Decian persecution (249-251) forces [[person:cyprian-of-carthage|Cyprian]] to flee; he writes extensively on the lapsed; controversy with Rome over treatment of apostates. Evidence: Cyprian, De Lapsis; On the Unity of the Church.

[[person:cyprian-of-carthage|Cyprian]] martyred under Valerian (258 AD); he is one of the most important early church fathers and martyrs; the Valerian persecution devastates North African leadership. Evidence: Pontius, Life of Cyprian; Acta Cypriani.
Carthaginian church recovers under Gallienus' peace (260); [[person:cyprian-of-carthage|Cyprian's]] writings remain enormously influential. Evidence: General records.
Carthaginian church growing during Great Peace; [[person:mensurius|Mensurius]] is bishop preceding Caecilian. Evidence: General records.

[[person:mensurius|Mensurius]] bishop; [[person:caecilian|Caecilian]] is deacon; the Donatist controversy is brewing over the handling of scriptures during the Diocletian persecution (traditores). Evidence: Augustine, Against the Donatists.

Diocletian persecution (303-305) causes the traditores controversy; [[person:mensurius|Mensurius]] is accused of handing over scriptures; seeds of Donatist schism planted. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8; Augustine.

Donatist schism erupts in Carthage (311-312) when [[person:donatus-of-carthage|Donatus]] contests [[person:caecilian|Caecilian's]] ordination by Felix of Aptunga (a traditor); Council of Arles (314) upholds Caecilian. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5.
North African church deeply divided between Donatists and Catholics; Nicaea (325) is attended by both sides. Evidence: Optatus of Milevis, De Schismate Donatistarum.
Donatist movement dominates much of North African Christianity; the schism continues despite imperial attempts at reunion. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate.
Donatists dominate North African Christianity; Council of Carthage (345/348) attempts reconciliation. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate.

[[person:donatus-of-carthage|Donatus]] exiled c.347 by Emperor Constans; the Donatist schism remains North Africa's central ecclesiastical crisis; Augustine will later combat it. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate.

[[person:paul|Paul]] has his hair cut at Cenchreae after a vow (Acts 18:18). [[person:phoebe|Phoebe]] is described as a diakonos (minister/deacon) of the church at Cenchreae and a prostatis (patron/helper) (Romans 16:1-2). She carries Paul's letter to the Romans. Evidence: Acts 18:18; Romans 16:1-2.
Cenchreae church continues; as the eastern port of Corinth, it is a hub for eastern trade and travel, with Christians moving through regularly. Evidence: Romans 16:1-2.
Cenchreaean bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Cenchreae church attested; Phoebe the deakoness is one of the most important named female church leaders in the NT. Evidence: Romans 16:1-2.
Bishop of Chalcedon attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Cirta church probable; Tertullian's writings implicitly reference wider North African Christianity. Evidence: Tertullian; general records.
Cirta church attested; Cyprian of Carthage's letters reference Numidian bishops during the Decian controversy. Evidence: Cyprian, Epistles.
Cirta church attested; Decian persecution forces lapsi issues across Numidia. Evidence: Cyprian, Epistles.

Cirta church attested; bishop [[person:paul-of-cirta|Paul of Cirta]] is one of the traditors who surrendered scriptures during Diocletian's persecution — this becomes a flashpoint for the Donatist schism. Evidence: Optatus, Against Donatists 1.14.

The Diocletian persecution is the flashpoint for the Donatist schism; at a synod in Cirta (305 AD), traditores bishops (those who surrendered scriptures) ordain a new bishop — [[person:donatus-of-casae-nigrae|Donatus of Casae Nigrae]] leads opposition. Evidence: Optatus, Against Donatists 1.14.

Cirta is a center of the Donatist controversy (311 AD); Constantine retakes Cirta and renames it Constantina in his honor. The city has two rival bishops: Catholic and Donatist. Evidence: Optatus; general records.

Constantina (formerly Cirta) church attested with split Catholic/Donatist communities; the Donatist controversy is addressed at the Council of Arles (314) and Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records; Optatus.
Constantina church attested; Donatist controversy continues; Augustine later refutes Donatism from Hippo. Evidence: General records.

Constantina church attested; the synod of Cirta (305 AD) that produced the traditors crisis is directly responsible for the Donatist schism — one of the most significant controversies in 4th-century North African Christianity. Evidence: Optatus, Against Donatists.
Colonia Agrippina church probable; Irenaeus's Gallic mission suggests spread toward the Rhine. Evidence: General inference.
Colonia Agrippina church probable; Tertullian references Christians among Germanic peoples broadly. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.
Colonia Agrippina church attested; bishop [[person:maternus|Maternus]] is recorded here and at Trier, attending the Council of Arles (314 AD). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop [[person:maternus|Maternus]] of Colonia Agrippina attends the Council of Arles (314 AD) — the first named bishop of Cologne. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop Maternus of Colonia Agrippina is traditionally listed among the bishops at Nicaea (325 AD). Evidence: Nicaea records (debated).

Colonia Agrippina church attested; bishop Maternus at Arles (314) and possibly Nicaea (325) makes Cologne one of the earliest documented episcopal sees north of the Alps. Evidence: Council of Arles records.

[[person:epaphras|Epaphras]] plants the Colossian church (Col 4:12-13); Paul writes the Letter to the Colossians from prison; [[person:philemon|Philemon]] and [[person:onesimus|Onesimus]] are also associated with Colossae (Philem 10). The city suffers a major earthquake c.60-61 AD. Evidence: Colossians 1:7; 4:12-13; Philemon.
Colossae is in decline following the earthquake; the church may have continued in the reduced city. Evidence: Pliny, Nat. Hist. 5.105 (earthquake reference).

Colossae continues to decline; the Christian community is probable but not independently attested after Paul's letter. Uncertainty: the city shrank significantly and later sources do not name Colossae separately from the Laodicean region. Evidence: general references.
Colossian church probable; Ignatius does not address Colossae (attests ongoing Laodicean presence instead). Evidence: General records.
Corduba is the capital of Baetica and a major Roman city; Christian presence probable by the late 1st/early 2nd century. Evidence: General inference; Tertullian references Spain broadly.
Corduba church attested by late 2nd century; Tertullian references Iberian churches. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.

Corduba church attested; [[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] (born c.256) is becoming active as a young man. He will be bishop of Corduba (~295-357 AD) and one of the most important western theologians. Evidence: General records.
Corduba church attested; [[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] is becoming the leading western bishop. Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] is bishop (~295-357); he is a leading western theological voice and a close adviser to Constantine I. Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] leads; he survives the Decian and Valerian persecutions. Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] continues as bishop. Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] is bishop; the Council of Elvira (c.306) addresses major disciplinary issues. Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] is bishop; he plays a key role at the Council of Elvira (c.306). Evidence: Council of Elvira records.

[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] presides at the Council of Elvira (~306); this council addresses major disciplinary and moral issues and is one of the earliest complete sets of church canons from the West. Evidence: Council of Elvira records.

[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] becomes [[person:constantine-i|Constantine's]] principal ecclesiastical adviser; he investigates the Donatist controversy in North Africa (c.313) and later presides at Nicaea. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 2.63.

[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] presides at Nicaea (325) as Constantine's representative; he may have proposed the Nicene creed formula 'of one substance' (homoousios). He is the most important western churchman of the early 4th century. Evidence: Nicaea records; Athanasius.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] continues as bishop; he attends the Council of Sardica (343-344). Evidence: General records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] continues; he resists Constantius II's Arian pressure until his final years. Evidence: General records.

[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] (d.357) is the dominant western church leader of the 4th century; his presiding at Nicaea and close relationship with Constantine shaped the Nicene settlement. Evidence: Athanasius, Hist. Ar.

[[person:paul|Paul]] spends 18 months in Corinth (~50-52 AD; Acts 18:11) with [[person:aquila|Aquila]] and [[person:priscilla|Priscilla]]; [[person:crispus|Crispus]] and [[person:gaius|Gaius]] among prominent converts; Gallio incident dates the stay precisely. Evidence: Acts 18:1-18; 1 Corinthians.

1 Corinthians (~54 AD) and 2 Corinthians (~56-57 AD) address serious divisions, sexual immorality, and questions of resurrection; the community is vibrant but troubled. Evidence: 1 Corinthians; 2 Corinthians.
Corinthian church well-established; Erastus the city treasurer (Romans 16:23) represents social diversity; Paul wrote Romans from Corinth. Evidence: Romans 16:23; Acts 20:2-3.
Corinthian church experiences factional disputes; 1 Clement (~96 AD) addresses Corinthian community, implying continued authority issues. Evidence: 1 Clement; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.16.
1 Clement (~96 AD) from Rome addresses factions in Corinth; the Roman church intervenes to restore deposed elders, demonstrating Rome's growing authority. Evidence: 1 Clement.
Corinthian church continues; 1 Clement is circulating and read aloud. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.16; 4.23.
Corinthian church attested; Ignatius does not address Corinth directly but implies a wider Pauline church network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
Corinthian church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
Corinthian church attested; approaching the era of Dionysius of Corinth who writes to multiple churches. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.

[[person:dionysius-of-corinth|Dionysius of Corinth]] (c.170) writes letters to multiple churches including Rome, Athens, Nicomedia, Sparta, Gortyna, Amastris, Cnossus; a key networked bishop. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.

[[person:dionysius-of-corinth|Dionysius of Corinth]] writes his famous letters (~c.170); he attests Paul and Peter's joint work founding both the Corinthian and Roman churches. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.

[[person:dionysius-of-corinth|Dionysius of Corinth]] writes letters to Rome (Soter), Crete, Athens, Sparta and others; key figure in 2nd-century ecumenical networking. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
[[person:dionysius-of-corinth|Dionysius]] active; writes to Eleutherus of Rome; corresponds on Easter controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.23.
Corinthian church continues as an important Achaean see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.
Corinthian church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.
Corinthian church continues as established Achaean see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Corinthian church continues; [[person:origen|Origen]] visited the area during his extensive travels. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23.
Corinthian bishop attends Council of Arles (314). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Corinthian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] begin first missionary journey at Salamis, Cyprus (Acts 13:4-12); proconsul [[person:sergius-paulus|Sergius Paulus]] believes. Barnabas was a native Cypriot (Acts 4:36). Evidence: Acts 13:4-12.
[[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] and John [[person:mark-the-evangelist|Mark]] revisit Cyprus (Acts 15:36-39) after splitting from Paul; church well-planted. Evidence: Acts 15:36-39.

[[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] traditionally martyred at Salamis, Cyprus (~61 AD); the Acts of Barnabas (5th c.) preserve this tradition. Uncertainty: dating and details legendary. Evidence: Acts of Barnabas; Eusebius.
Cypriot church has established episcopal see at Salamis; Barnabas venerated as founder. Evidence: Eusebius; episcopal lists.

Cypriot church probable; Jewish revolt in Cyprus (115-117 AD) devastates the island's Jewish population but effects on the Christian community are unclear. Uncertainty: specific impact on Christians undocumented. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.2.
A Cypriot bishop attends the Council of Arles (314), attesting an established episcopal see. Evidence: Council of Arles attendance records (314).
Cypriot bishop(s) attend Council of Nicaea (325); Cyprus is a functioning episcopal see. Evidence: Nicaea council attendance records.

[[person:epiphanius-of-salamis|Epiphanius of Salamis]] (born c.310-320) will later become bishop of Salamis (~367); the Cypriot church claims full autocephalous status which will be confirmed at Ephesus (431). Evidence: General records; Epiphanius biography.

Simon of Cyrene carries Jesus' cross (Mark 15:21); Cyrenean Jews witness Pentecost (Acts 2:10); Cyrenaean members of the Freedmen's synagogue in Jerusalem dispute with Stephen (Acts 6:9). Men from Cyrene plant the church in Antioch (Acts 11:20). Evidence: Acts 2:10; 6:9; 11:20; Mark 15:21.
[[person:lucius-of-cyrene|Lucius of Cyrene]] is listed as a prophet and teacher in the Antiochene church (Acts 13:1); he likely maintained ties to Cyrene. Evidence: Acts 13:1.

Cyrenaean church probable; city devastated by Jewish revolt of 115-117 AD (Kitos War) which significantly disrupts the Jewish and likely Christian communities. Uncertainty: Impact on Christian community unclear. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.2.
Cyrenaean church probable; city is rebuilding after the Jewish revolt of 115-117 AD. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.2.

Bishop of Cyrene attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); [[person:synesius-of-cyrene|Synesius of Cyrene]] (born ~370) will later be the most famous bishop from this area, but the church is already well established. Evidence: Nicaea records.
Cyrenaean church attested; the city has one of the earliest Gentile Christian mission connections in Africa via Acts. Evidence: Acts 11:20; Mark 15:21.

[[person:paul|Paul's]] conversion on road to Damascus (~33-35 AD) and baptism by [[person:ananias-of-damascus|Ananias]]; a Christian community pre-existed Paul's arrival. Evidence: Acts 9:1-22; Gal 1:17.
Paul escapes Damascus in a basket (Acts 9:23-25; 2 Cor 11:32-33); community continues under local leadership. Evidence: Acts 9:23-25; 2 Cor 11:32-33.

Damascus church continues as established community within the broader Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source documents Damascus specifically in this decade. Evidence: Implied in broader Syrian records.

Damascus church persists post-70 destruction of Jerusalem; likely absorbs some refugees from Palestinian Christian community. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church continues; Ignatius' letters imply a well-networked Syrian Christian community though they do not address Damascus directly. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles (implied Syrian network).
Damascus church part of Syrian network. Uncertainty: specific Damascus records absent for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church part of the broader Syrian episcopal network under Antiochene influence. Uncertainty: no independent primary source. Evidence: Antiochene records; general Syrian church tradition.
Damascus church continues as part of Syrian ecclesiastical network; Ignatius' letter corpus presupposes well-structured Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles (implied).
Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: specific records absent. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church continues; no independent primary documentation. Uncertainty: specific records absent. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church part of Syrian network; Justin Martyr and contemporaries attest broad Syrian Christianity but not Damascus specifically. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho.
Damascus church probable in Syrian episcopal network. Uncertainty: no specific documentation. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church part of Syrian episcopal network; Theophilus of Antioch attests broad Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Theophilus, Ad Autolycum.
Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church continues in Syrian network; Serapion of Antioch presupposes well-networked Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.
Damascus church part of Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church part of Antiochene Syrian network; Origen visits Syria broadly in this era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Evidence: General Syrian records; Antiochene tradition.
Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records.
Damascus church continues; Paul of Samosata controversy at Antioch (260s) will later affect broader Syrian church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29.
Damascus church probable; Decian persecution (249-251) affects the broader Syrian and Palestinian church network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39.
Damascus church probable; Decian persecution disrupts Syrian Christianity broadly. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39.
Damascus church probable; recovery after Valerian persecution (257-260); Gallienus edict of toleration. Evidence: General Syrian records.
Damascus church probable within Syrian network affected by Paul of Samosata controversy at Antioch. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30.
Damascus church part of Syrian network; Great Peace before Diocletian allows church growth. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
Damascus church probable; pre-Diocletian peace allows development. Evidence: General Syrian records.
Damascus church probable; Diocletian's Great Persecution (303-312) affects the entire Eastern church network including Syria. Evidence: Eusebius, De Martyribus Palaestinae.
Damascus church attested within Syrian episcopal network; Edict of Milan (313) brings freedom; Syrian churches publicly emerge. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5.
Damascus participates in the broader Syrian ecclesiastical post-Nicaea context as part of Antiochene network. Evidence: Council records via Eusebius.
Damascus church part of Syrian Nicene network; affected by Arian controversy developing at Antioch. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.
Damascus church part of Syrian network affected by Arian dominance at Antioch. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.
Damascus church an established see in post-Nicaea period; part of contested Syrian ecclesiastical landscape. Evidence: Sozomen; Theodoret.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] flee to Derbe from Lystra and preach the gospel, making many disciples (Acts 14:20-21). [[person:gaius-of-derbe|Gaius of Derbe]] later travels with Paul on his final journey (Acts 20:4). Evidence: Acts 14:20-21; 20:4.
Paul and Barnabas return to Derbe on their homeward journey, strengthening the disciples (Acts 14:21); Paul and Silas likely visit on the second journey (Acts 16:6). Evidence: Acts 14:21; 16:6.
Dura-Europos church probable by this decade; architectural renovation of a house into a Christian meeting place likely underway. Evidence: Archaeological evidence (Dura-Europos excavations).

The Dura-Europos house church (domus ecclesiae) is the earliest archaeologically known Christian house church, dated c.232-240 AD by inscription and stratigraphy; it contains baptistery paintings of Christ healing the paralytic, the women at the tomb, and David and Goliath. Evidence: Kraeling, The Excavations at Dura Europos (1967).
Dura-Europos house church active; the building has a dedicated baptistery with Christian wall paintings—among the earliest known Christian art. Evidence: Kraeling, Dura Europos excavations.
Dura-Europos house church continues; the city is under threat from Sasanid Persia. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.

Dura-Europos is captured and destroyed by the Sasanid Persians under Shapur I c.256-257 AD; the Christian house church is buried under Persian siege embankments, which ironically preserved it until modern excavation. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.
Eboracum church probable; Irenaeus references Britain broadly as a Christian land. Evidence: Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 1.10.2.
Eboracum church probable; Tertullian (c.197 AD) mentions Christians in Britain. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.

[[person:constantius-chlorus|Constantius I]] (father of Constantine) dies at Eboracum (306 AD); Constantine is proclaimed Augustus by the troops here — the starting point of his rise to become the first Christian emperor. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 1.21.

[[person:eborius|Eborius]], bishop of Eboracum, attends the Council of Arles (314 AD) — the first direct attestation of a bishop of York by name; one of the earliest named bishops in British history. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Eboracum bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Eboracum church attested; Eborius (bishop, 314 AD Council of Arles) is the earliest named bishop of Britain; Constantine's proclamation here (306 AD) set the stage for the Christianization of the empire. Evidence: Council of Arles records; Eusebius.

The Abgar Legend holds that [[person:king-abgar-v|King Abgar V]] of Edessa wrote to Jesus requesting healing and received a reply letter; after Jesus's death, the apostle [[person:thaddaeus|Thaddaeus]] (Addai) is said to have come to heal and evangelize the king. Eusebius claims to have found the original Aramaic correspondence in the Edessene royal archives (Ecc. Hist. 1.13). The historical core is uncertain but Edessa has one of the oldest attested Christian communities east of the Euphrates. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Doctrina Addai.

Edessa church early but dates uncertain; tradition holds [[person:addai|Addai]] founds the church and his successor [[person:aggai|Aggai]] continues the work. Evidence: Doctrina Addai; general records.

Tradition holds [[person:addai|Addai]]/Thaddaeus brought Christianity to Edessa from Jerusalem via the Abgar correspondence; scholarly consensus places first attestation much later. Uncertainty: Abgar legend first attested by Eusebius (Ecc. Hist. 1.13) c.313 AD; not contemporaneous. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Doctrine of Addai.
---

Edessa church continues; Palut (Paluth) is later bishop c.200 AD. Evidence: General records.

Tradition holds [[person:aggai|Aggai]] succeeds [[person:addai|Addai]] as bishop of Edessa; no contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: the tradition is entirely post-apostolic in attestation. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13.
---

Edessa church continues; Osrhoene is sometimes tributary to Rome, sometimes to Parthia. Evidence: General records.

Tradition continues through the Addai-[[person:aggai|Aggai]] succession; no contemporary documentation exists. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai.
---

Edessa church attested; Bar Daisan (Bardesanes, 154-222 AD) is born here and becomes a major Syriac Christian poet and gnostic-influenced thinker. Evidence: General records.

Tradition records [[person:palut|Palut]] as bishop of Edessa; scholarly opinion places his episcopate no earlier than c.200. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

Edessan tradition records [[person:palut|Palut]]; the earliest plausible attestation of Christianity in Osroene comes from the late 2nd century. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13.
---

Edessa church attested; Bar Daisan (Bardesanes) is active here, writing Syriac Christian hymns and philosophical dialogues. Evidence: General records.

No contemporary documentation of Christianity in Edessa in this decade. Uncertainty: All sources are retrospective. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13.
---

Edessa church attested; Bar Daisan (Bardesanes, 154-222 AD) is a major Syriac Christian poet who writes hymns, dialogues, and the Book of the Laws of Countries — a sophisticated defense of free will. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.

No contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: tradition entirely retrospective. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai.
---

Edessa church attested; Bar Daisan at court of Abgar VIII. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.

bardaisan-of-edessa|[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan of Edessa]]]] (154-222) is born; he will become an important Syriac Christian philosopher. The king of Edessa's conversion (trad.) suggests Christian presence, though dating is debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13.
---

Edessa church attested; Abgar VIII (c.177-212 AD) is widely considered the first Christian king in history — the first monarch to formally adopt Christianity as his faith. Evidence: Chronicles of Edessa; Segal, 'Edessa: The Blessed City'.

bardaisan-of-edessa|[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan of Edessa]]]] (154-222) grows up; Christianity is probably present in Edessa by mid-2nd century given the later attestation. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested; Palut is bishop c.200 AD. Evidence: General records.

[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan of Edessa]] (154-222) is active; he is a court theologian under King Abgar VIII; writes many Syriac hymns and theological works. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested; Tatian's Diatessaron (a gospel harmony in Syriac) becomes the standard gospel text in Edessa. Evidence: General records.

Christianity in Edessa attested by c.170-200 AD at the latest; [[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] is the most prominent early Christian figure. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30; Julius Africanus.
---

Edessa church attested; the Peshitta OT (Syriac translation) is associated with Edessa. Evidence: General records.

Christianity in Edessa is securely attested by c.170 AD; [[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] (154-222) and [[person:palut|Palut]] are leading figures; the royal court of Abgar VIII is Christianized. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30; Chronicle of Edessa.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan of Edessa]] is a major Syriac Christian philosopher; he writes The Book of the Laws of Countries; Julius Africanus visits Edessa. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

Edessa is an important center of Syriac Christianity; [[person:palut|Palut]] is bishop; [[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] writes extensively. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] continues active until his death (222); he writes on fate, free will, and astrology from a Christian perspective. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested; the Acts of Thomas (early Syriac text) is likely composed here. Evidence: General records.

[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] (d.222) and [[person:palut|Palut]] lead the Edessan Christian community; Edessa is a major center of Syriac Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

[[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] dies 222; Edessa is firmly established as a center of Syriac Christian learning. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

Edessa continues; Diocletian persecution broadly affects Eastern Christianity; Eusebius is copying the Abgar documents around this time. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Chronicle of Edessa.
---

Edessa church attested; Diocletian persecution. Evidence: General records.

Eusebius records the Abgar correspondence in his Ecclesiastical History (c.313), attesting Edessan Christianity's strong identity and tradition. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13.
---

Edessa church attested post-persecution. Evidence: General records.

Edessa participates in Nicaea as part of the Eastern church; the Syriac church is developing its own theological tradition. Evidence: Nicaea records.
---

Bishop of Edessa attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] (c.306-373) is growing up in Nisibis; he will become the greatest Syriac theologian; the Syriac church is a thriving center. Evidence: General records.
---

Edessa church attested; Ephrem the Syrian (c.306-373) is later associated with Nisibis and Edessa — the greatest Syriac theologian and hymn-writer. Evidence: General records.

Edessa is an important Syriac Christian center; [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] is active in Nisibis; he will come to Edessa after 363. Evidence: General records.
---

Edessa church attested. Evidence: General records.

Edessa is a major Syriac Christian center; the School of Edessa is developing; [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] is the foremost Syriac theologian of the age. Evidence: General records.
---

Edessa church attested; Edessa is the mother city of Syriac Christianity and its first Christian king (Abgar VIII, c.177-212 AD) makes it arguably the first Christian monarchy in history; the Syriac tradition it spawns reaches India, Persia, China, and Arabia. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Segal, 'Edessa'.

The Council of Elvira (~306 AD) is held here — the first major church council in the Western Roman Empire; 19 bishops and 26 priests from all parts of Spain attend; it issues 81 canons covering clerical celibacy, mixed marriages, lapsi, and excommunication — producing the earliest extant comprehensive Western canon law. Evidence: Council of Elvira canons.
