Elvira church attested; the Council of Elvira (~306 AD) was held here; its 81 canons are highly influential in the development of Latin church law. Evidence: Council of Elvira canons.
Bishop of Elvira attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Ossius (Hosius) of Córdoba is a key Spanish theologian at Nicaea. Evidence: Nicaea records.

Elvira church attested; the Council of Elvira (~306 AD) held here — with its 81 canons regulating priestly celibacy, mixed marriages, idolatry, and excommunication — is the earliest surviving comprehensive Western church council and a foundational document for Latin canon law. Evidence: Council of Elvira canons.
Emerita Augusta church attested by late 2nd century; Tertullian references Spanish churches broadly. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.

Emerita Augusta church attested; the lapsed bishop [[person:marcellus-of-merida|Marcellus of Merida]] is discussed by Cyprian of Carthage — one of the earliest named bishops of Spain and an important case in the Decian lapse controversy. Evidence: Cyprian, Ep. 67.
Emerita Augusta church continues after the Decian controversy; a new bishop is ordained. Evidence: Cyprian, Ep. 67.
Emerita Augusta bishop attends the Council of Elvira (c.306); the church is well-established in the capital of Lusitania. Evidence: Council of Elvira records.
Bishop of Emerita Augusta attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Emerita Augusta church attested; the case of bishop Marcellus (Cyprian, Ep. 67) makes Merida one of the earliest specifically documented Hispanic sees. Evidence: Cyprian, Ep. 67.
Bishop of Emesa attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

On the day of the Resurrection, the risen [[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus]] walks unrecognized with two disciples to Emmaus (~7 miles from Jerusalem), expounds the scriptures, then is recognized at the breaking of bread (Luke 24:13-35) — one of the most theologically rich resurrection appearances. The exact site is disputed between Latrun, Qubeibeh, Abu Ghosh, and Motza. Evidence: Luke 24:13-35.
Emmaus community probable; Origen identifies a site in this region. Evidence: General records.
Emmaus church attested by early 3rd century; Eusebius and the Onomasticon reference the site. Evidence: Eusebius, Onomasticon.
Emmaus church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Onomasticon.
Bishop of Emmaus area attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Eusebius of Caesarea documents the site. Evidence: Nicaea records.

Emmaus church attested; the Road to Emmaus appearance (Luke 24:13-35) — where the risen Jesus expounds scripture to two disciples and is recognized at the breaking of bread — is one of the foundational texts for Christian eucharistic theology and the hermeneutics of OT prophecy fulfilled in Christ. Evidence: Luke 24:13-35.

[[person:paul|Paul]] arrives in Ephesus on 3rd journey (~53-54 AD); [[person:aquila|Aquila]] and [[person:priscilla|Priscilla]] disciple [[person:apollos|Apollos]]; Paul spends over 2 years. Evidence: Acts 18:18-28; Acts 19.

[[person:paul|Paul]] spends 2+ years in Ephesus; silversmith riot (Acts 19:23-41); [[person:timothy|Timothy]] serves as resident leader; Paul writes 1 Corinthians from Ephesus. Evidence: Acts 19; 1 Cor 16:8.

[[person:timothy|Timothy]] serves as leader (1 Timothy 1:3); [[person:john-the-apostle|John the Apostle]] traditionally moves to Ephesus post-70 with Mary; Ephesus becomes central to Asian Christianity. Evidence: 1 Timothy 1:3; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.1.

[[person:john-the-apostle|John the Apostle]] traditionally resident in Ephesus in old age; Revelation addressed to the Seven Asian Churches (Rev 2:1-7 to Ephesus) from Patmos. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.23; Revelation 2:1-7.

[[person:john-the-apostle|John the Apostle]] traditionally dies at Ephesus in old age during Trajan's reign (~c.100 AD); his Gospel and letters are linked to this period and location. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.23.
[[person:ignatius-of-antioch|Ignatius of Antioch]] writes to the Ephesians on his way to martyrdom; the Ephesian church is praised for orthodoxy and hospitality. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Ephesians.
Ignatius' letter to Ephesus names their bishop [[person:onesimus|Onesimus]] (possibly the Onesimus of Philemon?); the church is active and well-organized. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Ephesians 1.
Ephesian church continuing; debates over gnosticism (Cerinthus tradition) and proto-orthodox theology continue in Asia Minor. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.
Ephesian church continues; important Asian metropolitan see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp of Smyrna]] visits Ephesus and confronts Marcion (the anecdote of 'I recognize the firstborn of Satan'). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.
Ephesian church attested as leading Asian see; involved in Quartodeciman Easter controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.
Ephesian church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22.
[[person:polycrates-of-ephesus|Polycrates of Ephesus]] is an important bishop who defends Quartodeciman practice in his famous letter to Victor of Rome (~c.190). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.

[[person:polycrates-of-ephesus|Polycrates of Ephesus]] writes to Victor of Rome defending Quartodeciman practice; lists distinguished Asians including Philip, John, Polycarp. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.
[[person:polycrates-of-ephesus|Polycrates]] continues as bishop; Ephesus is the leading see of the Province of Asia. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22-5.24.
Ephesian church attested; Bishop of Ephesus attends Council of Arles (314). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Ephesian bishop attends Nicaea (325); the city retains its importance as the premier Asian see. Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip the Evangelist]] preaches in Samaria (Acts 8:5-25) and Peter and John visit to confirm the conversions; Flavia Neapolis (modern Nablus, near ancient Shechem) is the main Samaritan city. Christian presence probable from the earliest Samaritan mission. Evidence: Acts 8:5-25.

[[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] is born in Flavia Neapolis c.100 AD (his own statement in 1 Apol. 1) — one of the most important 2nd-century Christian apologists; he grew up in a pagan Samaritan family and converted to Christianity as a young man. Evidence: Justin, 1 Apol. 1; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.
Neapolis church continues; [[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] grows up here and is educated; he converts and begins his philosophical-Christian career. Evidence: Justin, 1 Apol. 1.
Neapolis church continues; Justin Martyr is now active elsewhere (Ephesus, Rome). Evidence: General records.
Bishop of Neapolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Neapolis church attested; Justin Martyr (born here c.100 AD) is the most famous Christian apologist of the 2nd century and the first to produce a systematic defense of Christianity to Roman emperors. Evidence: Justin, 1 Apol. 1.

[[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus]] crosses the Sea of Galilee to Gadara (Gerasa in some MSS) and heals two men possessed by demons ('Legion'); the demons enter a herd of pigs that rush into the sea (Matt 8:28-34; Mark 5:1-20; Luke 8:26-39); the healed man becomes a missionary to the Decapolis ('Go tell what God has done for you' — Mark 5:19-20). Evidence: Matt 8:28-34; Mark 5:1-20.
Gadara church probable; the healed demoniac commissioned as a missionary to the Decapolis (Mark 5:20) would have included Gadara in his proclamation. Evidence: Mark 5:19-20; general records.
Gadara church attested; Origen identifies Gadara as the site of the swine miracle in his Commentaries on John. Evidence: Origen, Comm. John 6.41.
Bishop of Gadara attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Gadara church attested; the healing of the Gadarene demoniacs (Mark 5:1-20) is one of Jesus's most dramatic miracles; the healed man commissioned to 'tell what the Lord has done for you' (Mark 5:19) is the prototype of the Christian missionary who bears witness in his own community. Evidence: Mark 5:1-20.
Bishop of Gangra attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

The Council of Gangra (c.340-341 AD) is held here — it condemns the excessive asceticism of Eustathius of Sebaste (who advocated abandoning marriage and private property) and issues 20 canons defending the goodness of marriage, meat-eating, and ordinary church attendance. Evidence: Council of Gangra canons.

Gangra church attested; the Council of Gangra (c.340 AD) held here is a landmark in defining the 'middle way' of early Christian asceticism — affirming both monastic and married life as valid paths, against extreme encratite movements. Evidence: Council of Gangra canons.

[[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip the Evangelist]] is directed by an angel to the road from Jerusalem to Gaza (Acts 8:26) where he encounters and baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch, a court official of the Kandake of Ethiopia; Philip is then transported to Azotus and preaches north to Caesarea. Evidence: Acts 8:26-40.
Gaza church continues; it is part of the broader Judean Christian network connected to Jerusalem. Evidence: Acts 8:40.
Gaza church continues; Origen references Christian communities in this area. Evidence: General records.
Bishop of Gaza attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Gaza church attested; the conversion of the Ethiopian eunuch on the road to Gaza makes this one of the earliest attested evangelism events in Acts, connecting the gospel to Africa. Evidence: Acts 8:26-40.

Gerasa church attested by the early 3rd century; the city later (4th century) has over 15 churches — one of the densest concentrations of early Christian architecture anywhere. Evidence: Archaeological evidence; general records.
Bishop of Gerasa attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Gerasa church attested; church construction intensifies post-Nicaea. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.

Gerasa church attested; Gerasa ultimately has more than 15 identified early Christian churches, making it one of the most archaeologically rich Christian sites in the ancient Near East. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.

[[person:paul|Paul]] leaves [[person:titus|Titus]] in Crete to 'set in order what remains and appoint elders in every city' (Titus 1:5), implying multiple Christian communities across the island by ~57 AD. Gortyna is the provincial capital of Creta et Cyrenaica. Paul's ship stops at Fair Havens in Crete (Acts 27:8-12). Evidence: Titus 1:5; Acts 27:8-12.
[[person:titus|Titus]] organizes the Cretan churches, appointing elders in every city per Paul's instruction. Evidence: Titus 1:5-9.
Gortyna church continues with appointed elders. Evidence: General records.
Gortyna church continues; [[person:philippus-of-gortyna|Philippus]], bishop of Gortyna (c.160-180) writes treatises against heresies. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.21-23.
Gortyna church continues; [[person:philippus-of-gortyna|Philippus of Gortyna]] writes against Marcionites. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.25.
Gortyna church attested; [[person:philippus-of-gortyna|Philippus of Gortyna]] is mentioned by Eusebius as a significant Cretan bishop and apologist. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.21.
Bishop of Gortyna attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Gortyna church attested; Paul's commission to Titus to appoint elders 'in every city' of Crete (Titus 1:5) makes this one of the earliest examples of organized church planting across an entire region. Evidence: Titus 1:5.

Bishop of Heliopolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Constantine orders construction of a church at Heliopolis to counteract the dominant pagan temples. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine; Nicaea records.
Heliopolis church attested; Constantine builds a church here and attempts to suppress the famous prostitution cult associated with the Venus temple. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.58.

Heliopolis church attested; Baalbek represents Christianity's toughest frontier — establishing a community in the shadow of the greatest pagan temple complex in the eastern Mediterranean; Constantine's personal intervention here underscores its symbolic importance for Christianizing the empire. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.58.
[[person:epaphras|Epaphras]] plants churches in Hierapolis, Colossae, and Laodicea (Col 4:13); Philip the Apostle is later associated by tradition with Hierapolis. Evidence: Colossians 4:13.

Hierapolis church attested via Paul's Colossian letter; tradition holds [[person:philip-apostle|Philip]] the Apostle died and was buried here (Polycarp of Smyrna references Philip at Hierapolis). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.31.
[[person:philip-apostle|Philip]] the Apostle reportedly dies and is buried at Hierapolis; Eusebius cites Polycrates of Ephesus for this tradition. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.31.

[[person:papias-of-hierapolis|Papias of Hierapolis]] (bishop c.100-130) is active; he is a contemporary of Polycarp of Smyrna and claims to have known the Apostle John. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.

[[person:papias-of-hierapolis|Papias of Hierapolis]] is bishop; he compiles his Exposition of the Dominical Oracles—five books of early Jesus traditions—and advocates a millennial kingdom. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.39.
[[person:papias-of-hierapolis|Papias of Hierapolis]] is bishop; his Exposition preserves traditions about Matthew and Mark writing their gospels. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.39.
[[person:papias-of-hierapolis|Papias of Hierapolis]] continues as bishop. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.39.
[[person:papias-of-hierapolis|Papias of Hierapolis]] dies ~130; his writings survive partially through Eusebius and Irenaeus. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.39.

[[person:apollinaris-of-hierapolis|Apollinaris of Hierapolis]] is bishop (~170 AD); he writes an Apology to Marcus Aurelius and works against the Montanist movement. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.27.
[[person:apollinaris-of-hierapolis|Apollinaris of Hierapolis]] is bishop; he writes a Paschal treatise and is active in the Quartodeciman controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.27.
[[person:apollinaris-of-hierapolis|Apollinaris of Hierapolis]] opposes Montanism, which had strong Asia Minor roots. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.27; 5.19.
[[person:apollinaris-of-hierapolis|Apollinaris of Hierapolis]] continues as an important anti-heretical voice. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.27.
Hierapolis bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Hippo Regius church attested by the late 2nd century; Tertullian references North African churches broadly. Evidence: Tertullian; general records.
Bishop of Hippo Regius attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); the Donatist controversy continues. Evidence: Nicaea records.
Hippo Regius church attested; Augustine of Hippo (354-430) will become bishop here and one of the greatest theologians of the ancient church. Evidence: General records.
Hispalis church attested; the Council of Elvira (~306 AD) includes bishops from Baetica including the Hispalis region. Evidence: Council of Elvira acts.
Hispalis church attested; the Council of Elvira (~306 AD) — the first regional church council in the West — involves bishops from across Baetica. Evidence: Council of Elvira acts.
Hispalis church attested; bishops from Hispalis attend Council of Arles (314 AD). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Bishop of Hispalis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] preach in Iconium on the first missionary journey (Acts 13:51-14:6); they make many converts but face Jewish-led opposition and flee to Lystra. Evidence: Acts 13:51-14:6.
[[person:paul|Paul]] revisits Iconium on the second missionary journey (Acts 16:2); [[person:timothy|Timothy]] (from this region) joins Paul's team. Evidence: Acts 16:1-6.
Iconian church continues; the Acts of Paul and Thecla (2nd c. apocryphal text) is set partly here, reflecting local Christian memory. Evidence: Acts 14; Acts of Paul and Thecla.
Iconian church continues; Thecla traditions strengthen local Christian identity. Evidence: Acts of Paul and Thecla.
Iconian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Iconian church attested; Paul's founding mission and the Thecla tradition make Iconium a major site of early Christian memory. Evidence: Acts 14; Acts of Paul and Thecla.

Jerusalem is founding locus; [[person:james-the-just|James the Just]] leads as first bishop; Acts 2-12 attest continuous community from Pentecost. Uncertainty: dates of individual apostolic departures vary. Evidence: Acts 2-12; Gal 1:18-19.

Apostolic Council (~49 AD; Acts 15) under [[person:james-the-just|James]] decides Gentile admission; [[person:paul|Paul's]] second Jerusalem visit confirmed by Gal 2. Uncertainty: council date debated 48-50 AD. Evidence: Acts 15; Gal 2:1-10.

[[person:paul|Paul's]] final Jerusalem visit (~58 AD) and [[person:james-the-just|James]]' martyrdom (62 AD) mark transition; [[person:simeon-bar-cleophas|Simeon bar Cleophas]] succeeds as bishop. Evidence: Acts 21-24; Josephus, Ant. 20.9.1.

Roman destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) scatters community; church reportedly fled to Pella before siege; [[person:simeon-bar-cleophas|Simeon bar Cleophas]] leads in exile/return. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5; 3.11.

Reconstituted community under [[person:simeon-bar-cleophas|Simeon bar Cleophas]] post-70; Jewish-Christian character continues. Uncertainty: community size and exact composition after 70 unclear. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.11-3.32.
[[person:simeon-bar-cleophas|Simeon bar Cleophas]] continues as bishop; Eusebius records the Jerusalem episcopal succession list for this era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.32.
[[person:simeon-bar-cleophas|Simeon bar Cleophas]] martyred c.107 by governor Atticus; succession of Jewish-Christian bishops continues. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.32; 4.5.
Succession of Jewish-Christian bishops at Jerusalem continues; Eusebius records the full episcopal list. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5.
Jewish-Christian episcopate continues approaching Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135). Uncertainty: exact bishop sequence debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5.

Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135) leads Hadrian to raze Jerusalem, found Aelia Capitolina, and install first Gentile bishops; Jews including Jewish Christians barred from the city. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.6; 4.12.
Gentile bishops continue at Aelia Capitolina; church now Gentile in composition; community rebuilds under Roman colony status. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12.
Gentile church at Aelia Capitolina develops; Justin Martyr references Jerusalem in his apologetic writings from Rome. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12.

[[person:narcissus-of-jerusalem|Narcissus of Jerusalem]] emerges as celebrated bishop famous for Easter vigil miracle (water to oil). Uncertainty: dates of his episcopate vary slightly. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.12; 6.9.
[[person:narcissus-of-jerusalem|Narcissus]] leads; Jerusalem involved in Quartodeciman Easter controversy alongside other Eastern sees. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.12; 5.23-5.25.
[[person:narcissus-of-jerusalem|Narcissus]] faces controversy causing temporary retirement; subsequently restored as bishop. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.9-6.11.

[[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander of Jerusalem]] joins the very elderly [[person:narcissus-of-jerusalem|Narcissus]] as co-bishop c.212 (overlap decade). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.10-6.11.

[[person:origen|Origen]] visits Jerusalem c.215-216; [[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander]] and Narcissus invite him to preach, causing controversy with Demetrius of Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.11; 6.19.
[[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander]] leads; [[person:origen|Origen]] preaches at Jerusalem and Caesarea by invitation of local bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
[[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander]] continues as bishop; involved in Palestinian synods active in this period. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19; 6.23.
[[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander]] maintains close relations with Origen now settled permanently in Caesarea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.27-6.30.
[[person:alexander-of-jerusalem|Alexander]] dies in prison during Decian persecution (~250); Mazabanes then Hymenaeus succeed. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39.
Decian persecution (249-251) kills Alexander; [[person:mazabanes|Mazabanes]] then [[person:hymenaeus|Hymenaeus]] bishop; community recovers. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 7.14.
Recovery after Valerian persecution (257-260); Gallienus edict of toleration (260) provides relief for Palestinian churches. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.13.
[[person:hymenaeus|Hymenaeus]] of Jerusalem attends synods concerning Paul of Samosata controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28-7.30.
The Great Peace before Diocletian allows church growth; [[person:zabdas|Zabdas]] then [[person:hermon|Hermon]] serve as bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
[[person:hermon|Hermon]] then [[person:macarius-i|Macarius I]] bishop; Macarius is the last bishop before Diocletian's Great Persecution (303). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
Diocletian's Great Persecution (303-312) strikes Palestine severely; Eusebius witnesses and records Palestinian martyrdoms firsthand. Evidence: Eusebius, De Martyribus Palaestinae.
[[person:macarius-i|Macarius I]] serves as persecution ends; Edict of Milan (313) brings religious freedom; church begins public recovery. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5.
[[person:macarius-i|Macarius]] of Jerusalem attends Nicaea (325) and secures imperial support for construction of Church of Holy Sepulchre (~326). Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.25-3.40.

[[person:helena|Helena's]] pilgrimage (~326-328) and dedication of Church of Holy Sepulchre (335) transform Jerusalem into a major pilgrimage center. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.25-3.43.
[[person:cyril-of-jerusalem|Cyril of Jerusalem]] begins catechetical lectures; Jerusalem grows as pilgrimage destination and theological center. Evidence: Cyril of Jerusalem, Catechetical Lectures.
[[person:cyril-of-jerusalem|Cyril of Jerusalem]] (bishop ~350) codifies catechetical practice; city firmly established as a major Christian see. Evidence: Cyril, Catechetical Lectures; Procatechesis.

[[person:peter|Peter]] raises Tabitha/[[person:tabitha|Dorcas]] from the dead at Joppa and stays with [[person:simon-the-tanner|Simon the Tanner]]; sees the vision of clean/unclean animals leading to Cornelius baptism. Evidence: Acts 9:36-43; 10:5-23.
---

Joppa is the NT's most pivotal coastal city for the Gentile mission: Peter raises Tabitha (Dorcas) here (Acts 9:36-43) and while staying at Simon the Tanner's house receives the vision of clean/unclean animals (Acts 10:9-16) that leads him to baptize the Gentile centurion Cornelius in Caesarea — the theological break-through for the Gentile church. Evidence: Acts 9:36-43; Acts 10:1-48.
Joppa church continues following Peter's ministry; no specific primary documentation for this decade. Evidence: Implied continuation from Acts 9-10.
Joppa church probable; Paul passes through the coastal region on his journeys. Evidence: Acts 27:3 (coastal Judaea route).
Joppa church likely disrupted by Roman-Jewish War and destruction (~68-70 AD); community status uncertain post-war. Uncertainty: no documentation. Evidence: General Palestinian records.
Joppa church probable in post-war Palestinian context. Uncertainty: no primary source documentation. Evidence: General Palestinian records.
Joppa probable; part of Palestinian episcopal network. Evidence: General Palestinian records.
Joppa probable; approaching Bar Kokhba revolt which reshapes Palestinian Christianity. Evidence: General Palestinian records.
Joppa church attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Joppa church attested; Peter's vision here (Acts 10:9-16) — that no food is unclean — opens the door to the universal Gentile mission and is the theological pivot of early Christianity's transition from a Jewish sect to a world religion. Evidence: Acts 10:9-16; Acts 10:34-48.
Bishop of Laodicea (Syria) attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
[[person:epaphras|Epaphras]] founds the Laodicean church (Col 1:7; 4:12-13); Paul writes a letter to Laodicea (Col 4:16; now lost or identified with Ephesians). Evidence: Colossians 4:12-16.
Revelation 3:14-22 addresses Laodicea as 'lukewarm'; admonished for self-sufficiency and lack of spiritual fervor; the community is well-established but complacent. Evidence: Revelation 3:14-22.
Laodicean church continues; part of the broader Asian episcopal network. Evidence: Revelation 3:14-22 (implied continuation).
[[person:sagaris|Sagaris]] bishop of Laodicea martyred under Marcus Aurelius (~c.166); Melito of Sardis references this event. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.
[[person:sagaris|Sagaris]] martyred (~166); Laodicea is an important Asian see in the Quartodeciman controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.
Laodicean bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bishop of Leptis Magna attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Christianity in Roman Britain is probable by the late 2nd/early 3rd century; Tertullian (~197 AD) mentions Britains as reached by Christianity. Uncertainty: Tertullian may be rhetorical exaggeration. Evidence: Tertullian, Adversus Judaeos 7.

Christianity probable in Roman Britain; Origen (~c.240) also mentions Britain having Christianity. Uncertainty: rhetorical nature of sources. Evidence: Origen, Homilies on Ezekiel 4.1; Tertullian, Adversus Judaeos 7.
Christianity probable in Roman Britain; Londinium is the provincial capital. Evidence: Origen, Homilies (mentions Britain).

Christianity probable in Britain; Alban of Verulamium may have been martyred c.209 or c.304 (dates debated). Uncertainty: dating of Alban's martyrdom is heavily disputed. Evidence: Bede, Ecclesiastical History 1.7.
Christianity probable in Britain; Alban of Verulamium martyred c.304 (scholarly date for his martyrdom). Evidence: Bede, Ecc. Hist. 1.7.
Diocletian persecution affects Britain; [[person:alban|Alban]] of Verulamium likely martyred c.304; Aaron and Julius of Caerleon also martyred. Evidence: Bede, Ecc. Hist. 1.7.

Three British bishops attend Council of Arles (314): [[person:restitutus-of-london|Restitutus of London]], [[person:eborius|Eborius]] of York, [[person:adelfius|Adelfius]] of Caerleon; this is the first solid documentary attestation of Christianity in Britain. Evidence: Council of Arles records (314).
British bishops represented at Nicaea (325) though evidence is indirect; the Church in Britain is an established part of the Western church. Evidence: Nicaea records.

The Christian community at Lyon was founded by missionaries from Asia Minor (Greek speakers); the earliest plausible estimate for Christian presence is mid-2nd century. Uncertainty: no primary source documents Lyon before the 177 martyrdoms. Evidence: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne (177).

[[person:pothinus|Pothinus]] of Lyon (a Greek-speaker) arrives as bishop; the community is primarily Asian Greek in origin. Uncertainty: dates of Pothinus' arrival debated. Evidence: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne (177).

[[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] arrives in Lyon from Asia Minor as presbyter; [[person:pothinus|Pothinus]] is aging bishop; the community is growing. Evidence: Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. (autobiographical sections).

The Martyrdoms of Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) under Marcus Aurelius; [[person:pothinus|Pothinus]] (bishop, 90+ years old), [[person:blandina|Blandina]] (slave), Attalus and others martyred; [[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] carries letter to Rome. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1-5.3.

[[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus of Lyon]] becomes bishop (~177); writes Against Heresies (Adversus Haereses, c.177-185) - the most comprehensive anti-gnostic work of the early church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.4; 5.20.

[[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] continues as bishop; writes Demonstration of the Apostolic Preaching; mediates between Rome and Asian churches on Easter dating. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.
[[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] dies c.202; successors lead Lyon; the Lyonese church remains a significant Gallican center. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.
Lyon church attested; Bishop of Lyon attends Council of Arles (314). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Lyon bishop attends Nicaea (325); important Western see. Evidence: Nicaea records.
Lyon is an established Western see with the legacy of Irenaeus and the 177 martyrdoms. Evidence: General records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] preach in Lystra (Acts 14:6-20); Paul heals a lame man; crowds try to worship them as Hermes and Zeus; Paul is stoned and left for dead but recovers. [[person:timothy|Timothy]] (from Lystra), his mother [[person:eunice|Eunice]] and grandmother [[person:lois|Lois]] are believers here. Evidence: Acts 14:6-20; 16:1-3; 2 Tim 1:5.
Paul returns to Lystra on the second missionary journey and recruits [[person:timothy|Timothy]] (Acts 16:1-3); the church has established elders. Evidence: Acts 16:1-3.
Lystran bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Lystran church attested; Paul's dramatic healing and stoning at Lystra are landmark episodes in the NT mission narrative. Evidence: Acts 14:6-20.

Tradition holds [[person:thomas|Thomas]] the Apostle arrived on the Malabar Coast c.52 AD and established seven churches; the Acts of Thomas (3rd c.) describes his mission to 'India.' Uncertainty: scholarly consensus places actual documented Syrian Christian presence no earlier than 3rd-4th century; the 52 AD date is traditional and not independently attested. Evidence: Acts of Thomas (3rd c.); Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.1.
Tradition claims Thomas converted Brahmin families and established communities; no independent historical evidence for 1st-century Christianity in Kerala. Evidence: Later tradition.
Claimed Christian presence continues per tradition; not independently attested in this decade. Evidence: Traditional accounts.
Claimed Christian presence continues per tradition. Evidence: Traditional accounts.

Trade between the Roman Empire and the Malabar Coast is well-attested (Periplus Maris Erythraei, c.50-70 AD); Christian traders could have arrived. Uncertainty: no evidence for a structured church community. Evidence: Periplus Maris Erythraei.
Claimed Christian presence. Evidence: Traditional accounts.
Claimed Christian presence. Acts of Thomas (c.225 AD) tells of Thomas's Indian mission. Evidence: Acts of Thomas.
Claimed Christian presence. Evidence: Acts of Thomas.

Syrian Christian missionaries likely arrive via trade routes by the late 3rd century; the Persian Church of the East has connections to India. Uncertainty: specific date and route unclear. Evidence: Cosmas Indicopleustes (6th c.) references earlier tradition.

Christian presence in Malabar attested by early 4th century; the tradition of Knayi Thoma (Thomas of Cana) bringing 72 families from Persia (trad. 345 AD) may reflect actual Syrian migration. Evidence: Later tradition and archaeological evidence of trade.

Tradition holds that [[person:knayi-thoma|Knayi Thoma]] (Thomas of Cana) arrives c.345 AD with 72 Syriac-speaking Christian families from Persia, establishing a distinct community in Kerala. Evidence: Later tradition.

Malabar Christian community attested; the St. Thomas Christians (Kerala Christians) trace their apostolic origin to Thomas and their Syrian connection to the Church of the East. Evidence: Later tradition; Cosmas Indicopleustes (6th c.).

[[person:paul|Paul]] is shipwrecked on Malta (Acts 28:1-10); he heals [[person:publius|Publius's]] father and other sick islanders; the islanders are initially friendly. Tradition holds the Maltese church was founded by Paul during his winter stay (c.60 AD). Evidence: Acts 28:1-10.

Tradition holds [[person:publius|Publius]] (whose father Paul healed) became the first bishop of Malta; later tradition says he later became bishop of Athens. Uncertainty: tradition is late but widely held in Maltese Christianity. Evidence: Acts 28:7-10; later tradition.

Massilia is the major port city of southern Gaul; Christians likely arrive via eastern sea trade routes by the late 2nd century, connected to the broader Gallic church around Lyon. Uncertainty: no direct primary source attests Massilia before the 3rd century. Evidence: Plausible inference from trade routes and Gallic church expansion.
Massilia church probable; the nearby martyrdoms at Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) suggest a broad Gallic Christian network in the region. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1.

Massilian church is attested by the early 3rd century; the city's role as a major Mediterranean port makes early Christian contact via sea trade highly probable. Evidence: Tertullian references Gallic churches broadly.

Christian presence in Mediolanum probable by mid-2nd century; the city is a major northern Italian hub on important road networks; Paul writes generally to 'all of Italy' (Heb 13:24). Uncertainty: no specific NT or early source attests Mediolanum before 3rd century. Evidence: General inference from Roman church expansion.
Mediolanum church attested by mid-2nd century; Irenaeus of Lyon broadly references northern Italian churches. Evidence: General records.

[[person:merocles|Merocles]] of Milan attends the Council of Rome (313) and the Council of Arles (314) — the earliest named bishop of Milan in historical sources. Edict of Milan (313) is associated with this city. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Milanese bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Mediolanum is a major western imperial city. Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:auxentius|Auxentius]] of Milan (bishop ~355-374) is appointed by Constantius II as an Arian bishop; the Council of Milan (355) forces Western bishops to condemn Athanasius. Ambrose of Milan (bishop 374-397) will transform this see. Evidence: Athanasius, Hist. Ar. 76.

Memphis (biblical Noph/Moph; modern Mit Rahina) is the ancient capital of Egypt; the Christian tradition holds that the Holy Family passed through the Memphis area during the Flight to Egypt (Matt 2:13-15), and multiple Coptic traditions locate holy sites along this route. Christian community probable by mid-1st century given Alexandria's proximity and the large Jewish diaspora population. Evidence: Matt 2:13-15; Coptic tradition.
Bishop of Memphis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Memphis church attested; the Coptic tradition holds that the Holy Family traveled as far south as Asyut in Upper Egypt, with Memphis as a key waypoint; the Church of Abu Sarga (Cairo) marks one traditional site. Evidence: Coptic tradition; Matt 2:13-15.

[[person:paul|Paul]] summons the Ephesian elders to Miletus and delivers his farewell speech (Acts 20:17-38) — one of the most important addresses to church leaders in the NT. He warns of coming persecution and false teachers. Paul also notes he left Trophimus sick at Miletus (2 Tim 4:20). Evidence: Acts 20:17-38; 2 Tim 4:20.
Milesian bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Miletus church attested; Paul's farewell speech to the Ephesian elders here is the longest speech in Acts and a landmark text on church leadership. Evidence: Acts 20:17-38.

Georgian tradition claims Andrew the Apostle preached in Iberia; this tradition is late and unattested before the medieval period. Uncertainty: apostolic founding tradition is not historically corroborated. Evidence: Later Georgian tradition only.
[[person:nino-of-cappadocia|Nino of Cappadocia]] (c.296-338 AD) arrives in Iberia as a slave girl or refugee and begins preaching Christianity. Evidence: Life of Nino (Kartlis Tskhovreba).
[[person:nino-of-cappadocia|Nino of Cappadocia]] continues missionary activity in Iberia; her healing of Queen Nana gains royal attention. Evidence: Life of Nino.

[[person:nino-of-cappadocia|Nino of Cappadocia]] heals [[person:king-mirian-iii|King Mirian III]] who was lost in a forest; he converts to Christianity c.326-337 AD (precise date debated). Iberia becomes officially Christian, making it among the first states to do so after Armenia. Evidence: Life of Nino; Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10.

[[person:king-mirian-iii|King Mirian III]] requests bishops and priests from Constantine I; the first bishop of Iberia is ordained; the Svetitskhoveli Cathedral site (Mtskheta) is established. Iberia is officially Christian c.327-337 AD. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10; Life of Nino.

The Iberian church is fully established; [[person:nino-of-cappadocia|Nino of Cappadocia]] dies c.338 AD and is venerated as Equal-to-the-Apostles; Mtskheta becomes the see of the Catholicos of all Georgia. Evidence: Life of Nino.

Mtskheta is the ecclesiastical capital of Iberia (Georgia); Nino's conversion of King Mirian III makes Georgia (along with Armenia) one of the first officially Christian states in history. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10; Life of Nino.

The centurion Julius finds a ship at Myra sailing for Italy, so [[person:paul|Paul]] and other prisoners board it (Acts 27:5-6) on the voyage to Rome (~60 AD); this attests at minimum a brief apostolic presence in this Lycian port. Evidence: Acts 27:5-6.
Myra church attested; [[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] (later venerated as 'Saint Nicholas' / 'Santa Claus') is born c.270 AD and becomes bishop of Myra. Evidence: General records.
Myra church attested; [[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] is bishop; he is known for generosity and care for the poor. Evidence: General records.
Myra church attested; [[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] is bishop. Evidence: General records.
Myra church attested; [[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] is reportedly imprisoned during Diocletian's persecution but survives. Evidence: General records.
Myra church attested post-persecution. Evidence: General records.

[[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] is traditionally said to attend the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) and vigorously defend the Nicene faith — the famous legend says he struck Arius during debate (historically uncertain). Evidence: Nicaea records; hagiographic tradition.
Myra church attested; [[person:nicholas-of-myra|Nicholas of Myra]] dies c.343 AD; veneration begins. Evidence: General records.

Myra church attested; Nicholas of Myra (c.270-343 AD), whose tomb here becomes a major pilgrimage site, is the historical figure behind the legend of Saint Nicholas / Santa Claus — bishop, miracle-worker, Nicaea defender, and patron of sailors, children, and gift-giving across the Mediterranean world. Evidence: Acts 27:5-6; hagiographic tradition.
Naissus church attested; Constantine I is born here (~272 AD) to [[person:constantius-chlorus|Constantius I]] and Helena; the city gains imperial significance. Evidence: General records.
Naissus bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bishop of Narbo attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Nazareth is Jesus's hometown where he was raised (Matt 2:23; Luke 4:16; '[[person:jesus-of-nazareth|Jesus of Nazareth]]'); he reads from Isaiah in the synagogue and is rejected (Luke 4:16-30); his family lives here. An early Jewish Christian community persists in Nazareth into the 3rd century. Evidence: Luke 4:16-30; Matt 2:23.
Nazareth Jewish Christian community continues; the desposynoi (blood relatives of Jesus) keep the community active. Evidence: Julius Africanus; general records.

Nazareth community continues; Nazareth is venerated as Jesus's hometown from the earliest Christian period and is one of the most significant pilgrimage sites in the Holy Land. Evidence: Luke 4:16-30; Matt 2:23.

Nazianzus church attested; [[person:gregory-the-elder|Gregory the Elder]], father of Gregory of Nazianzus (the Theologian), converts from the Hypsistarian sect and is ordained bishop of Nazianzus c.328 AD after Nicaea. Evidence: Gregory Naz., Orat. 18 (on his father).
Nazianzus church attested. Evidence: General records.
Nazianzus church attested; [[person:gregory-the-elder|Gregory the Elder]] converts c.325-328 AD and becomes bishop. Evidence: General records.
Nazianzus church attested; [[person:gregory-the-elder|Gregory the Elder]] attends Nicaea or subsequent councils. Evidence: General records.
[[person:gregory-the-elder|Gregory the Elder]], bishop of Nazianzus, attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Nazianzus church attested; [[person:gregory-the-elder|gregory]]-of-nazianzus|[[person:gregory-of-nazianzus|Gregory of Nazianzus]]]] (the Theologian, c.329-390 AD) is born here — he becomes one of the greatest theologians of the 4th century, co-architect of Nicene Trinitarian theology with Basil of Caesarea and Gregory of Nyssa. Evidence: Gregory Naz., autobiographical poems.
Nazianzus church attested. Evidence: General records.

Nazianzus church attested; [[person:gregory-the-elder|gregory]]-of-nazianzus|[[person:gregory-of-nazianzus|Gregory of Nazianzus]]]] is raised here and will later articulate the definitive Nicene doctrine of the Trinity in his Five Theological Orations (380 AD), earning him the title 'the Theologian' — one of only three Christians in Orthodox tradition given this title (along with John the Apostle and Symeon the New Theologian). Evidence: Gregory Naz., Orat. 27-31.

Neapolis (Naples) is one of the oldest and largest Greek cities in Italy, located on the Bay of Naples adjacent to Puteoli where Paul disembarks (Acts 28:13); Christian presence probable by mid-1st century given its proximity to the Puteoli landing and constant traffic with Rome. Evidence: General inference.

Neapolis church attested; Bishop [[person:januarius-bishop-of-benevento|Januarius]] of Benevento is martyred near Puteoli (305 AD) and his relics are later interred at Naples — he becomes the patron saint of Naples. Evidence: General records.
Bishop of Neapolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Christian presence in Pontus is probable by the late 1st century; 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Pontus among scattered diaspora Christians; Pliny's letters (c.111) describe Christians throughout Bithynia-Pontus. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1; Pliny, Ep. 10.96.
Neocaesarean church probable; Pontus is a significant province for early Christianity. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1.
Neocaesarean church attested; Pontus has confirmed Christian presence via Pliny's letters to Trajan (c.111). Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97.

[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] (Gregory the Wonderworker, born c.213) studies under Origen at Caesarea Maritima from ~233. His later role as bishop transforms Neocaesarea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.30.
[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] studies under Origen; his Panegyric to Origen (~238) is our main source for his education. Evidence: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Panegyric to Origen.

[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] returns to Pontus and is made bishop of Neocaesarea (~240). He reportedly found only 17 Christians on arrival and left 17 non-Christians when he died. Evidence: Gregory of Nyssa, Life of Gregory Thaumaturgus.

[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] is bishop; he is known for miraculous deeds (Thaumaturgus = Wonderworker) and for his Canonical Epistle which addresses moral and canonical issues. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28; Gregory of Nyssa, Life of Gregory.

[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] leads the church through the Decian persecution; his Canonical Epistle sets penance rules for those who lapsed or were captured during the Gothian invasion. Evidence: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Canonical Epistle.
[[person:gregory-thaumaturgus|Gregory Thaumaturgus]] continues as bishop; he attends the synods against Paul of Samosata (c.264-268). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28.
Gregory Thaumaturgus dies ~270; unnamed successors continue. The church he built is legendary in Pontus. Evidence: Gregory of Nyssa, Life of Gregory Thaumaturgus.
Neocaesarean church continues; the Council of Neocaesarea (314-315 AD) meets here and issues important canonical rules. Evidence: Council of Neocaesarea canons.
Neocaesarean bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); the Council of Neocaesarea (314-315) had already established a model for conciliar governance. Evidence: Nicaea records.
Neocaesarean church attested; Basil of Caesarea (born ~330) will eventually be connected to this region's church through his family. Evidence: General records.
Neocaesarean church attested; Gregory Thaumaturgus is venerated as the founder and patron of the Pontic church. Evidence: Gregory of Nyssa, Life of Gregory Thaumaturgus.
Nicaea is a significant Bithynian city; Christianity probable given Pliny's letter attesting widespread Christian presence in the province (~111-112 AD). Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97.
Nicaea church attested through Pliny's province-wide letter; an episcopal community exists in this important Bithynian city. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97.

Nicaea hosts the First Ecumenical Council (325 AD) under [[person:constantine-i|Constantine]]; 318 bishops gather; the Nicene Creed and homoousios formula are adopted against Arianism. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.6-3.14; Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.7.

[[person:pliny-the-younger|Pliny the Younger's]] letter to Trajan (c.111-112 AD) attests that Christians are numerous throughout Bithynia-Pontus province; Nicomedia is the provincial capital. Uncertainty: letter does not name Nicomedia specifically. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97.

[[person:pliny-the-younger|Pliny's]] letter to [[person:trajan|Trajan]] (c.111-112 AD) is the premier extrabiblical attestation of Christianity; Christians in Bithynia had abandoned their faith in the face of persecution. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97.
Nicomedian church continues as established community in provincial capital. Evidence: Implied from Pliny; general records.
Nicomedia is Diocletian's Eastern capital; [[person:anthimus-of-nicomedia|Anthimus]] is bishop; the Great Persecution will begin here in 303 AD. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8.
Diocletian's Great Persecution begins at Nicomedia (303 AD); the church building is destroyed; [[person:anthimus-of-nicomedia|Anthimus]] bishop martyred. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8.6.
[[person:eusebius-of-nicomedia|Eusebius of Nicomedia]] becomes bishop; he is an Arian sympathizer who will be key to the Arian controversy. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.4.

[[person:eusebius-of-nicomedia|Eusebius of Nicomedia]] supports Arius at Nicaea (325) and is exiled briefly; [[person:constantine-i|Constantine I]] is baptized by him on his deathbed (337). Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.5.

[[person:constantine-i|Constantine I]] dies at Nicomedia (337 AD) and is baptized by [[person:eusebius-of-nicomedia|Eusebius of Nicomedia]]; the Arian party dominates the city. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 4.61.
[[person:eusebius-of-nicomedia|Eusebius of Nicomedia]] dies c.341; Nicomedia remains under Arian influence. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.7.

[[person:paul|Paul]] instructs Titus to join him at Nicopolis where he plans to winter (Titus 3:12); the most probable identification is Nicopolis in Epirus (Augustus's victory city near Actium). A Christian community may have been established during Paul's stay. Uncertainty: multiple cities named Nicopolis exist. Evidence: Titus 3:12.
Nicopolis community probable following Paul's winter stay. Evidence: Titus 3:12.
Nicopolis bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Nicopolis church attested; Paul's mention of wintering here (Titus 3:12) is one of the few explicit geographical references to western Greece in the NT. Evidence: Titus 3:12.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] will become bishop c.300; he is one of the most important early Syriac Christian leaders. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Nisibis church attested; Jacob of Nisibis becomes bishop and is one of the great Syriac Christian leaders. Evidence: General records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] (bishop c.300-338) is a major Syriac bishop and ascetic; credited with miraculous defense of Nisibis against Persian sieges. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Nisibis church attested; Jacob of Nisibis is bishop. Evidence: General records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] bishop; he will defend the city against Persian sieges (338, 346, 350); celebrated as a wonder-worker. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Nisibis church attested; Ephrem the Syrian (c.306-373 AD) is born in Nisibis and studies under Jacob; he is the greatest Syriac theologian, poet, and hymn-writer, composing hundreds of madrasha (theological hymns). Evidence: General records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] bishop; [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] studies under him here; Nisibis is a major Syriac Christian center. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Nisibis church attested; Jacob of Nisibis attends Nicaea (325 AD). Evidence: General records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] attends Nicaea (325) as an important Eastern representative; he and [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem]] together make Nisibis a center of Syriac theology. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Jacob of Nisibis attends the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) and becomes an important voice for Nicene Christianity in the Syriac East. Evidence: Nicaea records; general records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] defends the city against Persian siege (338 AD); [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] is an active theologian and hymnwriter here. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.11.
---

Nisibis church attested; Jacob of Nisibis dies 338 AD; Ephrem the Syrian runs the school of Nisibis. Evidence: General records.

[[person:jacob-of-nisibis|Jacob of Nisibis]] dies c.338; [[person:babu|Babu]] succeeds; [[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] continues his prolific theological and hymnographic work. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 2.26.
---

Nisibis church attested; Ephrem the Syrian is active in the School of Nisibis, the premier theological institution of the Syriac world. Evidence: General records.

[[person:ephrem-the-syrian|Ephrem the Syrian]] is the dominant Syriac theologian; Nisibis will fall to Persia in 363 AD forcing Ephrem to relocate to Edessa. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 4.29.
---

Nisibis church attested; the School of Nisibis becomes the most important Christian theological institution outside the Greco-Roman world; it produces the Syriac theological tradition that reaches Persia, India, China, and Arabia. Evidence: General records.

Ostia is Rome's principal seaport and the gateway through which Paul would have passed en route to Rome (c.60 AD; cf. Acts 28:13-16 — Paul arrives via Puteoli but Ostia is the main port gateway); early Christian community probable given the constant flow of travelers and merchants from the East. Evidence: General inference.

Ostia church attested by mid-1st century; as the largest port in the western Mediterranean it has substantial Christian traffic and settlement. Archaeological evidence of a Christian gathering space found at Ostia. Evidence: Archaeological evidence.
Bishop of Ostia attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Ostia church attested; Monica (mother of Augustine) dies here c.387 AD — Ostia's connection to Augustine highlights its importance in later church history. Evidence: General records.

Oxyrhynchus church probable; the earliest known Christian private letter (P.Oxy. 8.1162) dates to c.300 AD but fragments of the Gospel of Thomas (P.Oxy. 1, 654, 655) found here may originate from 2nd-century use. Evidence: P.Oxy. fragments.

Oxyrhynchus church attested. The Oxyrhynchus papyri (discovered 1896-1907) from this site include P.Oxy. 1 (sayings of Jesus), P.Oxy. 654, 655 (Gospel of Thomas fragments), and hundreds of NT manuscripts and early Christian documents. Evidence: P.Oxy. collections.

Oxyrhynchus church attested; P.Oxy. 8.1162 (c.300 AD) is the earliest known Christian private letter in Greek, showing that ordinary Oxyrhynchites were Christian by this period. Evidence: P.Oxy. 8.1162.
Bishop of Oxyrhynchus attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Oxyrhynchus church attested; the site is the single most productive source of early Christian papyri ever discovered — over 500,000 fragments including NT texts, apocryphal gospels, liturgical documents, and private Christian letters, making it unparalleled for understanding grassroots early Christianity. Evidence: P.Oxy. collections.

Palmyra church attested; Queen Zenobia (r.267-272 AD) conquers Egypt, Syria, and Asia Minor, creating the short-lived Palmyrene Empire; she patronizes [[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]], the bishop of Antioch whose Monarchian/dynamic monarchian theology is condemned by synods (268 AD). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-30.
Bishop of Palmyra attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Palmyra church attested; Zenobia's patronage of Paul of Samosata (bishop of Antioch condemned 268 AD) makes Palmyra uniquely entangled with the era's greatest Christological controversy before Nicaea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-30.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] travel across Cyprus from Salamis to Paphos (Acts 13:6-12); at Paphos they encounter Bar-Jesus/Elymas (a Jewish magician and false prophet), whom Paul strikes blind. The proconsul [[person:sergius-paulus|Sergius Paulus]] believes, making him one of the first high Roman officials to convert to Christianity. Evidence: Acts 13:6-12.
Bishop of Paphos attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Paphos church attested; the conversion of Sergius Paulus at Paphos (Acts 13:6-12) is one of the most dramatic conversion accounts in Acts, demonstrating Christianity's penetration into Roman imperial administration. Evidence: Acts 13:6-12.

[[person:john-the-apostle|John]] is exiled to Patmos 'because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus' (Rev 1:9), traditionally under Domitian (c.95-96 AD); he receives the Revelation here. Patmos itself would have had a small resident Christian community connected to the Asian churches. Evidence: Revelation 1:9.
Patmos is the site of [[person:john-the-apostle|John's]] exile and the reception of the Revelation (~95-96 AD). Evidence: Revelation 1:9.
Patmos community continues; [[person:john-the-apostle|John]] writes or dictates the Revelation to the seven churches of Asia here. Evidence: Revelation 1:9-11.

[[person:john-the-apostle|John]] receives and writes the Revelation on Patmos (c.95 AD) under Domitian's reign; the visions address the seven churches of Asia. Evidence: Rev 1:9; Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 5.30.3.
Patmos community continues; John reportedly returns to Ephesus after Domitian's death and Nerva's recall of exiles. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.20.
Patmos community attested; the island is the place where John received the Revelation (c.95 AD), making it a sacred site for all Christian traditions. Evidence: Rev 1:9.

According to Eusebius (Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3), the Jerusalem Christian community fled to Pella in the Decapolis before the Roman siege of Jerusalem (66-70 AD), heeding Jesus's warning to flee (Matt 24:16; Luke 21:21). Uncertainty: Pella flight tradition is accepted by some scholars and questioned by others. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3.
The Pella community includes Jerusalem Jewish Christians who fled before the war; this community may have preserved early traditions. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3.
Pella church continues; Jewish Christians remain in Pella after the fall of Jerusalem (70 AD); some may have returned to Jerusalem after the war. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3.
Pella bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Pella church attested; the flight of Jerusalem Christians to Pella before 70 AD (Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3) is one of the most significant events in the transition from Jewish to Gentile Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3.

Pepuza is declared the 'New Jerusalem' by [[person:montanus|Montanus]] (c.156-172 AD), a charismatic Phrygian prophet; together with [[person:priscilla|Priscilla]] and [[person:maximilla|Maximilla]] (female prophets), he founds the Montanist ('New Prophecy') movement here. The movement emphasizes direct prophetic inspiration, strict asceticism, and an imminent Second Coming. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16-19; Epiphanius, Panarion 48-49.

Montanism spreads rapidly from Pepuza across Asia Minor; the proto-orthodox leadership in Asia holds synods to condemn the New Prophecy; Apollinaris of Hierapolis writes against it. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16; Apollinaris of Hierapolis.
Montanus, Priscilla, and Maximilla are dead by this period; Pepuza remains the Montanist center and pilgrimage destination. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18.

Pepuza remains the Montanist headquarters; Tertullian in Carthage becomes a Montanist sympathizer c.206 AD; the movement spreads to North Africa, Rome, and Gaul. Evidence: Tertullian, De Ecstasi; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18.
Pepuza continues as Montanist center; proto-orthodox church holds councils in Asia condemning Montanism. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16-19.
Pepuza continues as Montanist pilgrimage center; Tertullian writes pro-Montanist tracts from Carthage (~207-220 AD). Evidence: Tertullian, De Monogamia; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18.

Pepuza Montanist community attested; the Montanist movement founded here by Montanus, Priscilla, and Maximilla (~156 AD) is the most important prophetic charismatic movement in early Christianity and provoked the first major crisis about the cessation of prophecy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16-19.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] arrive at Perga in Pamphylia (Acts 13:13); John [[person:mark-the-evangelist|Mark]] leaves here and returns to Jerusalem — a departure that will later cause Paul and Barnabas to separate (Acts 15:36-41). Paul preaches the gospel in Perga on the return journey (Acts 14:25). Evidence: Acts 13:13-14; 14:25.
Perga church continues following Paul's preaching. Evidence: Acts 14:25.
Bishop of Perga attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Perga church attested; Paul's preaching here on both legs of his first journey (Acts 13:13; 14:25) and John Mark's dramatic departure here make Perga a significant NT crossroads. Evidence: Acts 13:13; 14:25.
Revelation 2:12-17 addresses Pergamon ('where Satan's throne is' = temple of Zeus/imperial cult); [[person:antipas|Antipas]] is named as a martyr. Evidence: Revelation 2:12-17.
Pergamene church continues; the city is the center of imperial cult in Asia, making Christian witness especially costly. Evidence: Revelation 2:12-17 (implied continued presence).
Pergamene church continues; Ignatius passes through Asia but does not write to Pergamon. Evidence: General Asian church records.
Pergamene church attested as established Asian see; part of the wider Johannine-tradition Asian network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.
Pergamene bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] states he went to 'Arabia' after his conversion (Gal 1:17), which many scholars interpret as the Nabataean kingdom with Petra as capital; no independent attestation of a church in Petra at this date. Uncertainty: 'Arabia' in Gal 1:17 is debated; it could refer to the Syrian desert region. Evidence: Galatians 1:17.

Christian presence at Petra is attested by the early 3rd century; Origen and Eusebius reference the church in Arabia. Bishops of Petra appear in 4th-century conciliar lists. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.

Petra church attested; [[person:beryllus-of-bostra|Beryllus of Bostra]] (Arabia) is corrected by Origen for Christological error (~244 AD), showing a vigorous church in Arabia Petraea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.33.
Bishop of Petra (Arabia) at Nicaea (325); bishops of Arabia are represented. Evidence: Nicaea records.

Philadelphia is one of the Seven Churches of Revelation (Rev 3:7-13); the community receives high praise—'I know your works. Behold, I have set before you an open door.' Planted likely via Ephesian mission of Paul. Evidence: Rev 3:7-13; Acts 19.
Philadelphia addressed in Revelation as a faithful community facing persecution from a 'synagogue of Satan'; praised as keeping the word of patient endurance. Evidence: Rev 3:7-13.
Philadelphian church continues as part of the Asian episcopal network. Evidence: Revelation implied continuation.
Ignatius of Antioch writes a letter To the Philadelphians (c.110), attesting an established episcopal community; he warns against those who follow Judaism. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Philadelphians.
Philadelphian church continues following Ignatius' correspondence. Evidence: General records.
Philadelphian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bishop of Philadelphia (Arabia) attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Philadelphia (Arabia) church attested; this city is distinct from the Lydian Philadelphia of Rev 3:7 — it is modern Amman, capital of Jordan. Evidence: General records.

[[person:paul|Paul's]] first European Christian community (Acts 16:12-40); [[person:lydia|Lydia's]] household is first European convert; Paul and [[person:silas|Silas]] imprisoned, earthquake frees them, jailer converted. Evidence: Acts 16:12-40; Philippians.
[[person:paul|Paul]] writes Philippians (~61 AD) while imprisoned, addressing a mature community; [[person:epaphroditus|Epaphroditus]] carried aid and the letter. Evidence: Philippians; Acts 20:6.
Philippian church continues post-Paul; the community is known for generosity and missionary support. Evidence: Philippians 4:15-18.
Philippian church continues; 1 Clement (c.96) and other early texts imply continued vitality of Macedonian churches. Evidence: 1 Clement; implied Pauline tradition.
Philippian church continues; one of Paul's most praised communities remains active in Macedonian Christianity. Evidence: Implied Pauline tradition.

[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp of Smyrna]] writes his letter to the Philippians (c.110-115), addressing their request for Ignatius' letters and correcting a deacon (Valens). Evidence: Polycarp, To the Philippians.
Polycarp's letter (c.110-115) addresses a corruption scandal involving deacon [[person:valens|Valens]]; community is well-established but struggling. Evidence: Polycarp, To the Philippians 11.
Philippian church continues as established Macedonian see. Evidence: Eusebius references Macedonian churches generally.
Philippian church probable. Evidence: General Macedonian records.
Philippian church attested; a bishop from Philippi attends Council of Arles (314). Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Philippian church attested; Macedonian bishops attend Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Philippopolis church attested; Origen possibly passes through on journeys to Arabia. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Bishop of Philippopolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] preach in the synagogue at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:14-52) on the first missionary journey; Paul's synagogue sermon here is the longest recorded in Acts; they are expelled by local authorities but make many converts. Evidence: Acts 13:14-52.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] return to Pisidian Antioch on the way back from their first missionary journey (Acts 14:21-23), strengthening the disciples and appointing elders. Evidence: Acts 14:21-23.
Church at Pisidian Antioch continues with appointed elders. Evidence: Acts 14:23.
Church continues; Thecla traditions (set partly in this region) strengthen local Christian identity. Evidence: Acts of Paul and Thecla.
Bishop of Pisidian Antioch attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Church attested; Paul's Acts 13 sermon at Pisidian Antioch is a landmark in the NT narrative of Gentile mission. Evidence: Acts 13:14-52.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and his companions sail from Tyre to Ptolemais and spend one day with the brothers there (Acts 21:7), attesting a Christian community at Ptolemais by c.57 AD. Evidence: Acts 21:7.
Bishop of Ptolemais attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Ptolemais church attested; Paul's one-day stop here (Acts 21:7) is the NT attestation of the community. Evidence: Acts 21:7.

[[person:paul|Paul]] lands at Puteoli on his voyage to Rome (Acts 28:13-14) and finds brothers there; he stays seven days. This implies an established Christian community at Puteoli by c.60 AD. Evidence: Acts 28:13-14.
Puteolan church attested; Paul's landing and 7-day stay (Acts 28:13-14) makes this one of the earliest attested communities in Italy. Evidence: Acts 28:13-14.

Ravenna church attested; tradition holds [[person:apollinaris|Apollinaris]] (Sant'Apollinare) as the first bishop of Ravenna, sent by Peter himself — though historical evidence for this dates him no earlier than the 2nd century. Evidence: Passio Apollinaris; general records.
Bishop of Ravenna attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Ravenna church attested; though Ravenna becomes western imperial capital only in 402 AD, its ancient Christian community — traced by tradition to Saint Apollinaris (reputedly sent by Peter) — makes it one of the most theologically significant early Italian sees outside Rome. Evidence: Passio Apollinaris; general records.

[[person:paul|Paul's]] ship lands at Rhegium for one day (Acts 28:13) after leaving Syracuse, awaiting a south wind to proceed to Puteoli; this is a brief NT transit attestation. Evidence: Acts 28:13.
Rhegium church attested; Paul's one-day stop (Acts 28:13) links the church here to the apostle's journey to Rome. Evidence: Acts 28:13.

A Roman church existed before Paul's letter (~57 AD); tradition holds [[person:peter|Peter]] arrived ~42 AD but this is debated. Claudius expelled Jewish Christians (~49 AD; Suetonius). Evidence: Romans 1:8-15; Acts 18:2; Suetonius, Claudius 25.4.
Roman church well-established when Paul writes Romans (~57 AD); [[person:aquila|Aquila]] and [[person:priscilla|Priscilla]] return (Romans 16); Paul plans to visit. Evidence: Romans 16; Acts 18:2.

[[person:paul|Paul]] arrives under house arrest (~60-62 AD; Acts 28); both [[person:peter|Peter]] and Paul traditionally martyred under Nero (~64-68 AD). Evidence: Acts 28; 1 Clement 5:4-5; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.1.

[[person:linus|Linus]] (listed by Irenaeus as first after Peter) then [[person:anacletus|Anacletus]] lead the Roman church; the Flavian Amphitheatre is built on site of Nero's gardens where Christians were martyred. Evidence: Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 3.3.3; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.2.

[[person:clement-of-rome|Clement of Rome]] leads and writes his authoritative letter to Corinth (~96 AD); Roman church asserts growing authority over distant communities. Evidence: 1 Clement; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.16.

[[person:clement-of-rome|Clement's]] letter to Corinth (1 Clement, ~96 AD) demonstrates Roman church's assumed authority; written during Domitian's reign. Evidence: 1 Clement; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.34.

Roman church grows; [[person:hermas|Hermas]]' Shepherd composed in Rome; [[person:alexander-i|Alexander I]] bishop ~109-119; diverse community with Jewish and Gentile Christians. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.34; 4.3.
Ignatius of Antioch's letter To the Romans reveals deep reverence for the Roman church; he asks them not to hinder his martyrdom. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Romans; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.
[[person:telesphorus|Telesphorus]] bishop, later martyred under Antoninus Pius; Marcion arrives in Rome ~140 and is initially received. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.10.

[[person:hyginus|Hyginus]] bishop; [[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] settles in Rome and opens school (~c.150); [[person:valentinus|Valentinus]] and Marcion active in Rome as major theological challengers. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.

[[person:marcion|Marcion]] expelled from Rome (~144); [[person:pius-i|Pius I]] then [[person:anicetus|Anicetus]] bishop; [[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] writes Apologies; proto-orthodox identity defined against Marcionites and Valentinians. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11; 4.14.

[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] visits Rome and debates Easter dating with [[person:anicetus|Anicetus]]; they agree to disagree; [[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] martyred (~165); Hermas circulates widely. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14; 4.16.

[[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] martyred under Rusticus (~165); [[person:soter|Soter]] bishop; Roman church sends financial aid to other churches, demonstrating social prominence. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.19; 4.23.
[[person:eleutherus|Eleutherus]] bishop; [[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] carries letter from Lyon martyrs to Rome (177) inquiring about Montanism. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.4.

[[person:victor-i|Victor I]] (~189-199) bishop; attempts to excommunicate Eastern churches over Easter dating (Quartodeciman controversy), asserting Roman primacy; [[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] persuades restraint. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.23-5.25.

[[person:zephyrinus|Zephyrinus]] succeeds (~199); [[person:hippolytus|Hippolytus]] is active as theologian; Roman community diverse and theologically complex. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.25-5.28.

[[person:zephyrinus|Zephyrinus]] bishop; [[person:callixtus-i|Callixtus]] is archdeacon; [[person:hippolytus|Hippolytus]] opposes perceived modalism; multiple theological streams compete in Rome. Evidence: Hippolytus, Refutatio; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.21.

[[person:callixtus-i|Callixtus I]] bishop (~217-222); [[person:hippolytus|Hippolytus]] sets himself up as rival bishop; the first Roman schism over penitential policy. Evidence: Hippolytus, Refutatio 9.
[[person:urban-i|Urban I]] bishop; schism with [[person:hippolytus|Hippolytus]] continues; Roman community complex. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.22-6.23.

[[person:fabian|Fabian]] bishop (~236-250); remarkable election (dove alighting); [[person:origen|Origen]] visits Rome c.212; the Roman church is the wealthiest in the West. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.36.

[[person:fabian|Fabian]] martyred in Decian persecution (250); [[person:novatian|Novatian]] and [[person:cornelius|Cornelius]] contest succession (251); rigorist Novatianist schism over lapsed. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.43.

Novatianist schism (251) divides church over lapsed treatment; [[person:cornelius|Cornelius]] supported by Cyprian; [[person:stephen-i|Stephen I]] debates baptism of heretics. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.43-6.45; 7.1-7.3.

[[person:sixtus-ii|Sixtus II]] martyred in Valerian persecution (258) along with Lawrence; Gallienus' peace (260) allows recovery; [[person:dionysius-of-rome|Dionysius]] bishop (~259-268). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27.
[[person:felix-i|Felix I]] corresponds with Eastern bishops on Paul of Samosata controversy; Roman church asserts broad theological authority. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30.

[[person:caius|Caius]] bishop; relatively peaceful period for Roman Christianity before Diocletian's persecution; the church is the wealthiest and most prominent in the West. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
[[person:marcellinus|Marcellinus]] bishop; later accused of apostasy during Diocletian's persecution (debated by scholars). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32; Liber Pontificalis.

Diocletian persecution severely affects Rome; [[person:marcellinus|Marcellinus]] dies 304; [[person:marcellus-i|Marcellus I]] then [[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius]] serve briefly amid controversy over lapsed. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8; Liber Pontificalis.

[[person:miltiades|Miltiades]] bishop at Constantine's victory (312); [[person:sylvester-i|Sylvester I]] (314-335) serves at Edict of Milan; Council of Arles (314) with Rome's involvement; Lateran Palace given to the church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5.

[[person:sylvester-i|Sylvester I]] bishop during Nicaea (325); his legates Vito and Vicentius attend; Lateran and St. Peter's Basilicas under construction. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.7.

[[person:sylvester-i|Sylvester I]] dies 335; [[person:mark-the-evangelist|Mark]] bishop briefly (336); [[person:julius-i|Julius I]] (337-352) succeeds; St. Peter's Basilica dedicated. Evidence: Liber Pontificalis.

[[person:julius-i|Julius I]] defends [[person:athanasius-of-alexandria|Athanasius]] during his exile to Rome (339-342); Rome becomes the champion of Nicene orthodoxy in the West. Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos.

[[person:julius-i|Julius I]] dies 352; [[person:liberius|Liberius]] elected; later exiled by Constantius II for refusing to condemn Athanasius; Rome firmly Nicene despite imperial Arian pressure. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.

Paul states he has preached the gospel 'all the way from Jerusalem to Illyricum' (Romans 15:19); Salona is the capital of Dalmatia (Illyricum), making it the most likely center of any Pauline mission to the region. Uncertainty: Paul mentions the region but not Salona specifically. Evidence: Romans 15:19.
Salona church probable; Paul's reference to Illyricum in Romans 15:19 implies missionary activity in this province. Evidence: Romans 15:19; 2 Tim 4:10 (Titus goes to Dalmatia).
[[person:titus|Titus]] travels to Dalmatia (2 Tim 4:10), suggesting a mission in the province and possibly connections to Salona. Evidence: 2 Tim 4:10.
Bishop of Salona attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Salona church attested; Paul's reference to preaching in Illyricum (Romans 15:19) and Titus's mission to Dalmatia (2 Tim 4:10) give the Salonitan church apostolic-era roots. Evidence: Romans 15:19; 2 Tim 4:10.

[[person:philip-the-evangelist|Philip the Evangelist]] preaches in Samaria (Acts 8:4-25); [[person:simon-magus|Simon Magus]] encounters Philip and tries to buy the Spirit from [[person:peter|Peter]] and [[person:john-the-apostle|John]]. Evidence: Acts 8:4-25.
Samaritan church continues following Philip's and the apostles' ministry. Uncertainty: no specific documentation for this decade. Evidence: Implied continuation from Acts 8.
Samaritan Christian community probable; Samaria was an important region for early Jewish-Christian expansion. Evidence: Justin Martyr (Samaria native) attests Christian presence.
Samaritan church probable post-70; Justin Martyr was born in Samaria (~100 AD) and attests Christian presence. Evidence: Justin Martyr, 1 Apology 1.
[[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] born in Nablus/Neapolis (Samaria) c.100-114; attests Christian presence in Samaria during his youth. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dial. with Trypho 1.
Samaritan Christian community continues; [[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] (born in Neapolis, Samaria) represents this heritage. Evidence: Justin Martyr, 1 Apology 1.

[[person:justin-martyr|Justin Martyr]] (born Neapolis/Samaria c.100-114) explicitly attests Christianity in Samaria in his youth; Samaria is an active area of Christian preaching. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dialogue 1.
Samaritan Christianity attested through Justin Martyr's explicit references to Christians in Samaria in his apologetic works. Evidence: Justin Martyr, 1 Apology 53.
Bishop of Samaria area attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Samaria church attested; Philip's mission to Samaria (Acts 8:5-25) is the first major missionary expansion beyond Jewish territory, inaugurating the evangelization of the Samaritans — a half-Jewish people despised by mainstream Jews. Evidence: Acts 8:4-25.
Samosata church attested; [[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]] (bishop of Antioch c.260-268) is associated with this city. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27.

[[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]], bishop of Antioch (c.260-268), is from Samosata; he is condemned for his Christology (denying the personal pre-existence of the Logos). Multiple councils of Antioch are convened against him. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-30.
[[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]] is bishop of Antioch; his theological controversy impacts the Samosatan church and the broader Eastern church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-30.
[[person:paul-of-samosata|Paul of Samosata]] is condemned and deposed c.268 at the Council of Antioch; the Samosatan church continues under orthodox leadership. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30.
Bishop of Samosata attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Samosata church attested; Paul of Samosata (c.260-268 AD) is the city's most famous theologian, condemned for dynamic monarchianism — his case set important precedents for Christological orthodoxy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-30.

Sardis is one of the Seven Churches of Revelation (Rev 3:1-6); the community is rebuked for having 'a name that you are alive, but you are dead.' This implies an established but spiritually declining congregation by the late 1st c. Uncertainty: Paul may never have visited directly; planted via Ephesian mission. Evidence: Rev 3:1-6; Acts 19.
Sardian church addressed in Revelation as alive but dead; the 'few who have not soiled their garments' are noted. Evidence: Rev 3:1-6.
Sardian church continues as part of the Asian episcopal network. Evidence: Revelation implied continuation.
Sardian church continues as part of Asian episcopal network addressed by Ignatius. Evidence: General records.

[[person:melito-of-sardis|Melito of Sardis]] (bishop c.166-190) is active; he will become one of the most important Asian apologists and advocates of Quartodeciman Easter. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.

[[person:melito-of-sardis|Melito of Sardis]] is bishop; he writes numerous works including his Peri Pascha (Easter Homily), which is a vivid example of Asian homiletics and Quartodeciman practice. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.

[[person:melito-of-sardis|Melito of Sardis]] is bishop and major apologist; writes an apology to Marcus Aurelius. He defends Quartodeciman Easter practice and produces his renowned Peri Pascha. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.
[[person:melito-of-sardis|Melito of Sardis]] continues as bishop; his Peri Pascha is among the earliest complete Christian literary works preserved. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.26.
[[person:melito-of-sardis|Melito of Sardis]] dies ~190; the Sardian see continues under unnamed successors. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.
Sardian church continues; involved in Quartodeciman controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24.
Sardian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Bishop of Scythopolis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Eusebius of Caesarea is a key bishop in the Palestinian delegation. Evidence: Nicaea records.
