Sebaste church attested; the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste — 40 Roman soldiers of the XII Fulminata legion stationed here — refuse to sacrifice to pagan gods and are condemned to freeze to death on a frozen pond (c.311 AD); they are among the most celebrated early Christian martyrs. Evidence: Basil of Caesarea, Homily on the Forty Martyrs; general records.
Sebaste church attested; veneration of the Forty Martyrs begins immediately after their death; Basil of Caesarea writes a homily in their honor c.374 AD. Evidence: Basil of Caesarea, Hom. 19.
Bishop of Sebaste attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Sebaste church attested; [[person:eustathius-of-sebaste|Eustathius of Sebaste]] (c.300-379 AD) is bishop here — a major figure in early asceticism whose extreme practices were condemned at Gangra (~340 AD). Evidence: General records.

Sebaste church attested; [[person:eustathius-of-sebaste|Eustathius of Sebaste]] influences both Basil the Great and the ascetic tradition; the Council of Gangra (~340 AD) condemns his followers. Evidence: Council of Gangra canons.

Sebaste church attested; the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste (c.311 AD) are among the most universally venerated early martyrs in both Eastern and Western Christianity; their feast day (March 9) is observed by Orthodox, Catholic, and many Protestant traditions. Evidence: Basil of Caesarea, Hom. 19.

Christianity in Mesopotamia is probable by the early 2nd century via the Syriac tradition from Edessa; Seleucia-Ctesiphon as Parthian capital would attract Christian traders. Uncertainty: no contemporary documentation. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13 (Addai tradition implies Mesopotamian spread).
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon (twin cities: the Hellenistic Seleucia on the Tigris and Parthian Ctesiphon opposite) is the capital of the Parthian Empire and later Sassanid Empire; Christian presence probable by early 2nd century via merchants and missionaries from Edessa and Antioch. Evidence: General inference.

Christianity probable in Mesopotamia; the [[person:bardaisan-of-edessa|Bardaisan]] school at Edessa implies growing Syriac Christian presence in the region. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; the bishop here becomes the 'Papa' (patriarch/catholicos) of the Church of the East. Evidence: General records.

Christianity probable in Mesopotamia; Syriac Christianity of Edessa is by now well-attested implying spread into Parthian territory. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

Christianity attested in Mesopotamia by the late 2nd century; Seleucia-Ctesiphon is developing as the center of eastern Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30; Chronicle of Edessa.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

Christian community in Mesopotamia continues under Sassanid rule; [[person:papa-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai will become the first bishop to claim primacy over all Eastern Christians. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

Mesopotamian Christianity continues under Sassanids. Evidence: General records.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

Mesopotamian Christianity continues. Evidence: General records.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; Papa bar Aggai (c.310-329) reorganizes the Church of the East and asserts the primacy of his see. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.

[[person:papa-bar-aggai|papa]]-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai (bishop c.280-329) begins his long episcopate; he will claim primacy over all Eastern bishops, an assertion challenged by other sees. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

[[person:papa-bar-aggai|papa]]-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai continues; the Eastern church is developing its own independent structure under Sassanid rule. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested. Evidence: General records.

[[person:papa-bar-aggai|papa]]-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai continues; Diocletian's alliance with Rome against Persia increases Sassanid suspicion of Christians as Roman sympathizers. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; Shapur II launches major persecution of Christians (339-379 AD) — seeing Christians as potential Roman allies and taxing them punitively. Evidence: General records.

[[person:papa-bar-aggai|papa]]-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai continues; the Synod of Seleucia (410 AD will formalize the East Syriac church structure; [[person:shimun-bar-sabbae|Shimun bar Sabbae]] will succeed Papa. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; Papa bar Aggai reorganizes the Church of the East, holds the Synod of Seleucia (c.315) and asserts patriarchal primacy over all eastern churches. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.

[[person:papa-bar-aggai|papa]]-bar-aggai|Papa]] bar Gaggai leads; the Eastern church under Sassanids is geographically separated from Nicaea; not represented at Nicaea (325). Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; unlike western churches, the Church of the East does not participate in Nicaea (325) — it develops independently under Sassanid rule. Evidence: General records.

[[person:shimun-bar-sabbae|Shimun bar Sabbae]] succeeds Papa bar Gaggai; Constantine's favor of Christians in Rome makes Sassanid authorities more suspicious of Eastern Christians. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested; Shimun bar Sabbae (Catholicos) is martyred by Shapur II in 341 AD at the start of the great Sassanid persecution. Evidence: General records.

[[person:shapur-ii|Shapur II]] launches Great Persecution of Christians (339-379); [[person:shimun-bar-sabbae|Shimun bar Sabbae]] martyred 344 AD; Christianity is under severe official pressure in Sassanid empire. Evidence: Acts of Mar Shimun; Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 2.9.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested but under severe Sassanid persecution; Shapur II executes multiple Catholicoi in rapid succession. Evidence: General records.

Shapur II's Great Persecution (339-379) continues; multiple bishops martyred; tens of thousands of Christians killed; the church persists underground. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 2.9.
---

Seleucia-Ctesiphon church attested under Sassanid persecution; the Church of the East here is the mother church of an extraordinary missionary tradition that will reach India, Central Asia, China (Tang Dynasty), and Arabia — all without Roman imperial support. Evidence: Synodicon Orientale; general records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and [[person:barnabas|Barnabas]] are sent out by the church at Antioch and sail from Seleucia Pieria (the port of Antioch) to Cyprus on the first missionary journey (Acts 13:4); Seleucia Pieria is the harbor gateway to the great Syrian city. Evidence: Acts 13:4.
Bishop of Seleucia Pieria attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Seleucia Pieria church attested; this harbor is where Paul and Barnabas set sail on the first deliberate Gentile mission (Acts 13:4) — a pivotal moment in the expansion of Christianity beyond Syria. Evidence: Acts 13:4.
Bishop of Serdica attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Constantine uses Serdica as a base. Evidence: Nicaea records.

The Council of Serdica (343-344 AD) convenes here — bishops from the West (Nicene) and East (Arian-leaning) meet but split; the council issues important canons on episcopal appeals and jurisdiction that shape later church law. Evidence: Council of Serdica acts.

Serdica church attested; Constantine's affection for this city ('Serdica is my Rome') and the major council held here (343-344 AD) make it one of the most politically and theologically significant cities in 4th-century Christianity. Evidence: Council of Serdica acts; general records.

Sidon is a major Phoenician port; Jesus visits the region of Tyre and Sidon (Mark 7:24-31); early Christian presence probable given its position on the coastal trade route. Uncertainty: no explicit NT founding event at Sidon. Evidence: Mark 7:24.
Sidon church probable; Acts 12:20 mentions Sidon in proximity to regional politics. Evidence: Acts 12:20; general inference.
[[person:paul|Paul]] stops at Sidon on his voyage to Rome and is permitted to visit 'his friends' (Acts 27:3), attesting an established Christian community. Evidence: Acts 27:3.
Bishop of Sidon attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Sidonian church attested; Paul's stop at Sidon (Acts 27:3) attests a community welcoming enough to care for the apostle. Evidence: Acts 27:3.

Sinope is a major Pontic port city; Christian presence probable given 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Pontus broadly. Uncertainty: no direct 1st-century source attests Sinope specifically. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1.
Sinope church attested; Pliny's letters (c.111) reference Christians broadly in Pontus. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96.
Sinope church attested; Pliny's Pontic Christians reference. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96.

Sinope has an established church; Marcion of Sinope's father is said to be a bishop here. Marcion (born c.85-110) grows up in this Christian milieu before traveling to Rome. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Marc. 1.1.

[[person:marcion|Marcion]] of Sinope is active in Sinope before his excommunication and move to Rome (~139 AD). His radical Paulinism and rejection of the OT begins here. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Marc. 1.1; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.11.
Sinope church continues; Marcion's heritage makes this city important in the history of heresy and proto-orthodox response. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Marc.
Bishop of Sinope attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Sinope church attested; Marcion of Sinope (born here) is the most significant heretic of the 2nd century and shaped proto-orthodox responses to canon and scripture. Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Marc.
[[person:irenaeus-of-sirmium|Irenaeus of Sirmium]] is bishop; he is martyred under Diocletian (c.304 AD) — one of the best-documented Pannonian martyrs. Evidence: Acts of Irenaeus.
Bishop Irenaeus of Sirmium is martyred (304 AD) under Diocletian; he is an important martyr witness in the Latin West. Evidence: Acts of Irenaeus.
Bishop of Sirmium attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Sirmium church attested; martyr Irenaeus of Sirmium (304 AD) is one of the best-documented Pannonian martyrs; the city later hosts important councils during the Arian controversy. Evidence: Acts of Irenaeus.

Revelation 2:8-11 addresses the church at Smyrna; [[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] is associated with Smyrna from a very early age. The community is established and facing persecution. Evidence: Revelation 2:8-11.

Smyrnan church well-established; [[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] (c.69-155/156) is young but growing in prominence; likely shaped by Johannine tradition in the region. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp of Smyrna]] leading; Ignatius of Antioch writes to Smyrna and to Polycarp personally on his way to martyrdom. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Smyrnaeans; To Polycarp.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp of Smyrna]] is the leading bishop of Asia; Ignatius praises him highly in his personal letter. Evidence: Ignatius, To Polycarp; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] writes his Epistle to the Philippians (c.110-115); he is the foremost bishop of Asia. Evidence: Polycarp, To the Philippians; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] is the dominant figure of Asian Christianity; Irenaeus as a young man hears him preach. Evidence: Polycarp, To the Philippians; Irenaeus, Letter to Florinus.

[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] is aging but still active; [[person:irenaeus-of-lyon|Irenaeus]] as a youth hears him preach and remembers his words about John the Apostle. Evidence: Irenaeus, in Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.20.
[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] visits Rome to discuss Easter dating with Anicetus (~c.155); they agree to disagree; Polycarp martyred shortly after. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.14.

[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp of Smyrna]] is martyred (~155/156 AD); the Martyrdom of Polycarp is the earliest extant martyrdom account outside the NT; he refuses to deny Christ. Evidence: Martyrdom of Polycarp.

[[person:polycarp-of-smyrna|Polycarp]] martyred (~155/156); the Martyrdom of Polycarp circulates widely as an exemplary account; his successor leads Smyrna. Evidence: Martyrdom of Polycarp; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.15.
Smyrnan church continues in post-Polycarp era; the Martyrdom of Polycarp circulates and shapes martyrdom theology. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.15.
Smyrnan bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Smyrna is an established Asian see with the legacy of Polycarp's martyrdom shaping its identity. Evidence: General records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] and his escort stop at Syracuse for three days (Acts 28:12) on the voyage from Malta to Rome (~60 AD); this attests at minimum a brief Christian presence during the apostle's transit. Evidence: Acts 28:12.
Bishop of Syracuse attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Syracuse church attested; Paul's three-day stop (Acts 28:12) is the NT anchor for Sicilian Christianity; the city has the catacomb of San Giovanni, one of the largest early Christian catacombs outside Rome. Evidence: Acts 28:12.

Paul explicitly states his intention to visit Spain after Rome (Romans 15:24, 28); if he was released from his first Roman imprisonment (~62 AD) and visited Spain, Tarraco as the provincial capital would be a likely destination. Uncertainty: Paul's Spanish mission is widely debated; Clement of Rome references it (1 Clem 5) but non-specifically. Evidence: Romans 15:24, 28; 1 Clement 5.
Tarraco church probable; Tertullian references Christian communities in Spain. Evidence: General records.
Tarraco church probable; Irenaeus references Christian communities broadly including Iberia. Evidence: Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 1.10.2.
Tarraco church attested by the late 2nd century; Tertullian references Christian communities in Spain (c.197 AD). Evidence: Tertullian, Adv. Iud. 7.

[[person:fructuosus-of-tarragona|Fructuosus of Tarragona]] (bishop) is martyred in 259 AD during the Valerian persecution — the first named bishop and martyr of Spain attested in contemporary documents. His Acts of Martyrdom survive. Evidence: Acts of Fructuosus.
Tarraco church attested; the Council of Elvira (c.306 AD) is the first major Iberian church council and involves bishops from across Spain. Evidence: Council of Elvira records.
[[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]] is the leading Spanish bishop; Tarraco church attested post-persecution. Evidence: General records.

Tarraco bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); [[person:hosius-of-corduba|Hosius of Corduba]], Spain's most prominent bishop, presides at Nicaea under Constantine's direction. Evidence: Nicaea records.
Tarraco church attested; Fructuosus of Tarragona's 259 AD martyrdom is one of the best-documented 3rd-century martyrdoms in the West. Evidence: Acts of Fructuosus.

[[person:paul|Paul]] is a native of Tarsus (Acts 9:11; 21:39; 22:3) and returns here after his conversion and Jerusalem visit (Acts 9:30; Gal 1:21). Barnabas retrieves Paul from Tarsus to bring him to Antioch (Acts 11:25). Evidence: Acts 9:30; 11:25-26; Gal 1:21.
Tarsus has an established Christian community following Paul's time here; Paul passes through the region on missionary journeys (Acts 15:41; 18:23). Evidence: Acts 15:41.
Tarsian bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Tarsian church attested; Paul's birthplace makes it a city of founding Christian memory. Evidence: Acts 9:11; 21:39.
Thebes church probable; Anthony the Great (251-356 AD) begins his ascetic life in the Egyptian desert near the Thebaid. Evidence: Athanasius, Life of Anthony.

Thebes church attested; [[person:anthony-the-great|Anthony the Great]] (c.251-356 AD) withdraws to the desert near the Thebaid c.285 AD, living as a hermit and founding the Christian monastic/eremitic tradition. Evidence: Athanasius, Life of Anthony; Coptic tradition.
Thebes church attested; [[person:anthony-the-great|Anthony the Great]] living as a desert hermit draws disciples. Evidence: Athanasius, Life of Anthony.
Thebes church attested. Evidence: General records.

Thebes church attested; [[person:pachomius|Pachomius]] (c.292-348 AD) is born near Latopolis/Esna in Upper Egypt; he later founds the first organized cenobitic (communal) monastery at Tabennisi (~320 AD) just north of Thebes. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius.
Thebes church attested. Evidence: General records.

Thebes church attested; [[person:pachomius|Pachomius]] converts to Christianity c.313 AD after experiencing Christian charity during his conscripted military service. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius.

Thebes church attested; [[person:pachomius|Pachomius]] founds the first cenobitic (communal) monastery at Tabennisi c.318-320 AD — the mother of Christian monasticism as a structured communal institution. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius; Athanasius.

Thebes church attested; [[person:pachomius|Pachomius's]] Tabennisi monastery grows rapidly; the Pachomian monastic federation eventually includes 9 monasteries and 2 nunneries. Evidence: Nicaea records; Pachomian chronicles.
Thebes church attested; Pachomian monastic federation grows across the Thebaid. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius.

Thebes church attested; [[person:pachomius|Pachomius]] dies of plague in 348 AD; his monastic Rule influences Benedict of Nursia and all Western monasticism through John Cassian. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius.

Thebes church attested; the Thebaid region — with Anthony the Great's eremitic tradition and Pachomius's cenobitic monastic federation — is the birthplace of Christian monasticism, which becomes the most influential form of organized Christian life outside parish churches. Evidence: Athanasius, Life of Anthony; Coptic Lives of Pachomius.

[[person:paul|Paul]] founds church in Thessalonica (~49-50 AD; Acts 17:1-9); 1 Thessalonians (~50 AD) is the earliest surviving Pauline letter, written shortly after planting. Evidence: Acts 17:1-9; 1 Thessalonians.

2 Thessalonians addresses the community; [[person:aristarchus|Aristarchus]] and [[person:secundus|Secundus]] from Thessalonica accompany [[person:paul|Paul]] on his later journeys (Acts 20:4). Evidence: Acts 20:4; 2 Thessalonians.
Thessalonian church continues as important Macedonian see; [[person:aristarchus|Aristarchus]] tradition preserves link to Paul. Evidence: Acts 27:2; Colossians 4:10.
Thessalonian church well-established; one of the major Macedonian sees. Evidence: Implied by Pauline letters and Eusebius' records.
Thessalonian church well-established; 1 Clement and Ignatius' era implies active Macedonian churches. Evidence: Implied Pauline tradition; Eusebius.
Thessalonian church attested; an important Macedonian see with Pauline roots. Evidence: Episcopal lists.
Thessalonian church attested. Evidence: General Macedonian records.
Thessalonian church probable. Evidence: General Macedonian records.
Thessalonian bishop attends Council of Arles (314); established episcopal see. Evidence: Council of Arles records.
Macedonian bishop(s) attend Nicaea (325); Thessalonica is important Macedonian see. Evidence: Nicaea records.

Revelation 2:18-29 addresses Thyatira; [[person:lydia|Lydia]] (Acts 16:14) was from Thyatira, implying a Jewish presence there; a prophetess called 'Jezebel' is condemned. Evidence: Revelation 2:18-29; Acts 16:14.
Thyatiran church continues; the community has been admonished by Revelation about toleration of false prophecy. Evidence: Revelation 2:18-29.
Thyatiran bishop attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Thyatiran church attested; Lydia (Acts 16:14) from Thyatira is an early named Christian from this city. Evidence: Acts 16:14.

Tiberias is the capital built by Herod Antipas c.20 AD on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, named for Emperor Tiberius; the lake is called 'Sea of Tiberias' in John 6:1 and 21:1; Jesus reportedly avoided the city (built over a cemetery, unclean to Jews), but his ministry centers on its surrounding shores. Christian presence probable from the Galilean mission. Evidence: John 6:1; John 21:1; general records.
Tiberias church attested; Origen visits Palestine including this region. Evidence: General records.

Tiberias church attested; the Jewish-Christian Count (Comes) [[person:joseph-of-tiberias|Joseph of Tiberias]] receives permission from Constantine to build churches in Jewish cities including Tiberias c.330-335 AD (Epiphanius, Panarion 30.4-12). Evidence: Epiphanius, Panarion 30.4-12.

Tiberias church attested; [[person:joseph-of-tiberias|Joseph of Tiberias]], a converted Jew, builds churches in Tiberias and other Galilean cities by imperial permission c.330s. Evidence: Epiphanius, Panarion 30.4-12; Nicaea records.
Tiberias church attested. Evidence: Epiphanius, Panarion 30; general records.

Tiberias church attested; though Jesus reportedly avoided Tiberias itself, the Sea of Tiberias is the setting for some of his most important miracles (feeding of 5000: John 6:1; post-resurrection appearance: John 21:1); the city becomes the center of rabbinic Judaism while simultaneously hosting a growing Christian community. Evidence: John 6:1; 21:1.

Tolosa church attested; [[person:saturninus|Saturninus]] (Sernin) is the first attested bishop of Tolosa; tradition holds he is sent from Rome c.245-250 AD and martyred by being tied to a bull and dragged from the Capitol steps. Evidence: Passio Saturnini; general records.
Tolosa church attested; [[person:saturninus|Saturninus]] first bishop and later martyr (~250 AD). Evidence: Passio Saturnini; general records.
Tolosa church attested. Evidence: General records.

Tolosa church attested; [[person:saturninus|Saturninus]] is martyred c.250 AD — tied to a bull which drags him down the Capitol steps; he is the patron saint of Toulouse; the Basilica of St Sernin built over his tomb is one of the finest Romanesque buildings in Europe. Evidence: Passio Saturnini.
Bishop of Tolosa attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Tolosa church attested; the martyrdom of Saturninus (~250 AD) — tied to a bull that drags him down the Capitol steps — is one of the earliest documented martyrdoms in Gaul and establishes Tolosa as a major early Christian center between Arelate and Caesaraugusta. Evidence: Passio Saturnini.
Bishop of Tomis attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Trapezus (modern Trabzon) is the major port of the eastern Black Sea; Georgian/Pontic tradition holds that Andrew the Apostle preached here on his way through Pontus. Uncertainty: Andrew tradition in Pontus is late and unverifiable. Evidence: Later tradition.
Trapezus church probable via early Pontic missions; 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Pontus broadly. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1.
Trapezus church probable; Pliny's letters reference Christians throughout Pontus (c.111 AD). Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96.
Trapezus church probable. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96.
Bishop of Trapezus attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

[[person:paul|Paul]] receives the 'Macedonian vision' at Troas (Acts 16:8-11), redirecting his mission to Europe; [[person:silas|Silas]], Timothy, and [[person:luke|Luke]] (implied by 'we'-passage) sail from Troas to Philippi. Paul finds an 'open door' at Troas (2 Cor 2:12) on a later visit. Evidence: Acts 16:8-11; 2 Cor 2:12.

[[person:paul|Paul]] revisits Troas on his third missionary journey; the Eutychus episode occurs here (Acts 20:5-12) — a young man falls from a window and is restored. Paul celebrates the Lord's Supper here. Evidence: Acts 20:5-12.
Troas church continues; Ignatius of Antioch writes from Troas on his way to martyrdom (~110 AD). Evidence: Ignatius, To Polycarp; To Philadelphia written from Troas.
Trojan bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.
Troas church attested; the Macedonian vision at Troas is a pivotal moment in NT mission history bringing Christianity to Europe. Evidence: Acts 16:8-11.
[[person:paul|Paul]] visits the Tyrian disciples for 7 days on his third journey (Acts 21:3-6); the pre-existing community warns him not to go to Jerusalem. Evidence: Acts 21:3-6.
Tyrian church continues as part of Phoenician coastal Christian network. Uncertainty: no specific documentation for this decade beyond implied continuation. Evidence: General Phoenician records.
[[person:origen|Origen]] visits Tyre during his Syrian travels; it is an established episcopal see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19.
Tyrian church attested as functioning episcopal see in the Phoenician coastal network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.21.
Tyrian church attested as episcopal see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23.
Tyrian church attested; part of Phoenician episcopal network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29.
Tyrian church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.40.
[[person:origen|Origen]] moves to Tyre in his final years and dies there c.254; Tyrian church thus associated with the greatest early Christian scholar. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.1.
Tyrian church continues as established see. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.13.
Tyrian church attested in Phoenician network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28.
Tyrian church attested. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32.
Diocletian persecution affects Tyre as an important coastal city; Eusebius later delivers an oration at the dedication of Tyre's new basilica. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.4.

[[person:eusebius-of-caesarea|Eusebius]] delivers famous oration at dedication of Tyre's new basilica (~315 AD, Ecc. Hist. 10.4); [[person:paulinus-of-tyre|Paulinus of Tyre]] is bishop and a key ally of Eusebius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.4.
[[person:paulinus-of-tyre|Paulinus of Tyre]] attends Nicaea (325); Tyre has a functioning episcopal see with a recently dedicated basilica. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.4.
Tyrian church attested in post-Nicaea network. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 1.

Armenian tradition holds that Bartholomew and Thaddaeus (Jude) brought Christianity to Armenia in the 1st century; these claims are not independently attested before the 4th century. Uncertainty: founding tradition is late hagiography. Evidence: Later tradition only.
Armenian Christian tradition claims apostolic founding by Bartholomew and Thaddaeus; these traditions are not independently attested until the 4th century. Evidence: Later tradition.

[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] (born c.257 AD) is a key figure; he will convert the Armenian king and establish the Armenian church. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia (5th c.).

[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] is imprisoned by King Tiridates III (~287-298 AD) in the Khor Virap dungeon; he survives 13 years of imprisonment. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia.
[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] remains imprisoned; small Christian communities exist in Armenia, including some royal court contacts. Evidence: Agathangelos.

[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] is released from imprisonment; he heals [[person:king-tiridates-iii|King Tiridates III]] who then converts to Christianity (~301 AD). Armenia becomes the first kingdom to adopt Christianity as state religion. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia.

[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] is consecrated as bishop / Catholicos by the bishop of Caesarea Cappadocia; the Great Cathedral of Vagharshapat (later Etchmiadzin) is founded. Armenia is the first Christian state. Evidence: Agathangelos.

[[person:aristakes|Aristakes]], [[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator's]] son, represents Armenia at the Council of Nicaea (325); Armenia is firmly in the Nicene camp. Evidence: Nicaea records; Agathangelos.

[[person:gregory-the-illuminator|Gregory the Illuminator]] dies c.328-331; his son [[person:aristakes|Aristakes]] succeeds as Catholicos. The Armenian church is fully institutionalized. Evidence: Agathangelos.

[[person:aristakes|Aristakes]] is martyred c.333; [[person:vrtanes|Vrtanes]] (Aristakes' brother) becomes Catholicos; the Armenian church faces pressure from both Persia and Arianism. Evidence: General records.

The Armenian church continues under [[person:vrtanes|Vrtanes]] and [[person:husik-i|Husik I]]; Armenia is the world's first Christian state and the Armenian Apostolic Church becomes the national church. Evidence: General records.

Valentia church attested; [[person:vincent-of-saragossa|Vincent of Saragossa]] (the deacon from Caesaraugusta) is martyred here c.304 AD under the governor Dacian during Diocletian's persecution — he becomes the most celebrated martyr of Spain and patron of numerous cities. Evidence: Acts of Vincent; Prudentius, Peristephanon 5.
Valentia church attested; veneration of Vincent of Saragossa begins immediately; Augustine of Hippo writes a famous sermon on him. Evidence: Augustine, Serm. 274-277; general records.
Bishop of Valentia attends or is affected by Nicaea (325). Evidence: Nicaea records.

Valentia church attested; the martyrdom of Vincent of Saragossa (deacon from Caesaraugusta) here c.304 AD — commemorated by both Prudentius and Augustine — makes Valentia one of the most venerated martyrdom sites in the western Mediterranean. Evidence: Prudentius, Peristephanon 5; Augustine, Serm. 274.

Vienne is adjacent to Lyon in southern Gaul; Christian presence probable by the 160s as the Gallic church develops. Uncertainty: no direct source before the 177 martyrdom account. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1 (implies prior community).

The martyrs of Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) are described in Eusebius; Vienne Christians including [[person:vetius-epagathus|Vetius Epagathus]] and [[person:sanctus-of-vienne|Sanctus of Vienne]] suffer alongside the Lyon martyrs. [[person:blandina|Blandina]] and Bishop [[person:pothinus|Pothinus]] are central figures. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1.
Viennese church continues after the martyrdoms; Irenaeus of Lyon is closely connected. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1; 5.3.
Viennese church attested; the martyrs of Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) established this as a center of Gallic martyrial memory. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1.
